definitions.title: Definition Set 1
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/01 - group-1-definitions.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/01 - group-1-definitions.html

items.answer: The scientific study of societies and social interaction.
Latin for “all other things held constant”. Economists assume ceteris paribus to simplify their models.

definitions.title: Definition Set 2
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/02 - group-1-definitions.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/02 - group-1-definitions.html

items.answer: When a firm splits up its production process into smaller separate tasks, and assigns different workers to each of these tasks.

When a worker, firm or economy concentrates on producing a limited range of goods and services. 

E.g. Farmers specialise in producing fruits and veg, Domino’s specialises in producing pizzas, Saudi Arabia specialises in producing oil

items.prompt: Functions of money (DUMS, Donald Uses Money Shopping)
items.answer:
Deferred Payment

Unit of account

Medium of exchange

Store of value
You can borrow money to buy goods now, and then pay the money back in the future
Money helps us compare prices of different products

We can use money to trade goods and services.

E.g. Domino’s pays its workers money for their services, the workers then use the money to pay for the goods and services they want
You can store your money and save it for later.

items.prompt: Pros and cons of division of labour
items.answer:
ProsCons
Can increase productivity and output by specialising and using specialist equipment


Demotivation can decrease productivity and output


Can increase quality because workers master their craft


Demotivation can decrease quality because workers will take less pride in their work


Can decrease unit costs because workers are more efficient


Can increase absenteeism, staff turnover and recruitment costs


Can help firms save on training costs because each worker needs to be trained in fewer skills


Specialised workers will have only one skill so may end up unemployed if they lose their jobs

items.prompt: Pros and cons of specialisation and trade
items.answer:
ProsCons
Wider variety of goods and services from trade

If a region over-specialises and their specialist industry shuts down, workers will have no other work, leading to unemployment e.g. miners in the 1980s after the mines were shut down

Can increase overall output

Countries can over-specialise and deplete their natural resources e.g. Saudi Arabia is slowly running out of its oil reserves

Can increase trade

We become vulnerable because if people specialised in producing a certain good or service go on strike or stop trading, we’re left without the goods and services we need

E.g. when train drivers strike the rest of us can’t get to work or school

definitions.id: 14 - Shifts in demand income and price of other goods
definitions.title: Shifts in demand: income and price of other goods
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/14 - Shifts in demand income and price of other goods.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/14 - Shifts in demand income and price of other goods.html

items.answer:
For normal goods (e.g. Ben & Jerry’s ice cream) demand will increase when incomes rise, and demand will decrease when incomes fall.


For inferior goods (e.g. Sainsbury’s Basics ice cream) demand will increase when incomes fall, and demand will decrease when incomes rise.

For substitute goods (e.g. iPhones and Samsungs) demand for the first good (iPhone) will decrease when price of second good (Samsung) falls, and demand for the first good (iPhone) will increase when price of second good (Samsung) rises.


For complementary goods (e.g. iPhones and iPhone apps) demand for the first good (iPhone) will increase when price of second good (iPhone apps) falls, and demand for the first good (iPhone) will decrease when price of second good (iPhone apps) rises.

definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/15 - intro and calculation of YED.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/15 - intro and calculation of YED.html

items.answer:
YED measures how much quantity demanded will change in response to a change in income.

YED = %△Qd/%△Y

definitions.title: Types of YED
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/16 - Types of YED.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/16 - Types of YED.html

items.answer: For normal goods (e.g. Ben & Jerry’s ice cream), demand increases when income increases, which means the YED for normal goods is positive.
For inferior goods (e.g. Sainsbury’s basics ice cream), demand increases when income decreases, which means the YED for inferior goods is negative
When YED is between 1 and ∞﻿. Income elastic goods are very responsive to changes in income and are likely to be luxury goods e.g. a rolex
When YED is between 0 and 1. Income inelastic goods are unresponsive to changes in income, and are likely to be necessity goods e.g. bread

definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/17 - Formula and intro.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/17 - Formula and intro.html

items.answer:
XED measures how much quantity demanded of good A changes in response to a change in price of good B

XED formula = % change in quantity demanded (Qd) of A / the % in price (P) of B

definitions.title: XED types
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/18 - XED types.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/18 - XED types.html

items.answer:
Complements are goods which are used and bought together (e.g. iPhones and iPhone apps)

For complements, if the price of good B (iPhone apps) increases then demand for good A (iPhones) decreases.

XED will be negative.

Substitutes are goods which can replace one another (e.g. iPhones and Samsungs)

For substitutes, if the price of good B (Samsungs) increases then demand for good A (iPhones) increases.

XED will be positive.

Unrelated goods are goods which have nothing to do with each other (e.g. cacti and hover boards)

XED = 0 for unrelated goods

definitions.title: Group 1
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/24 - Group 1.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/24 - Group 1.html

items.answer: A market is at equilibrium when supply equals demand.
When the price is above the equilibrium price, the quantity supplied will exceed the quantity demanded leading to excess supply (or a surplus).
When the price is below the equilibrium price, the quantity demanded will exceed the quantity supplied, leading to excess demand (or a shortage).
The interaction of supply and demand to determine the price in a market.

SIR

Signalling: higher prices signal to producers that consumers want more of their goods, so producers supply more of them. Lower prices signal to producers that consumers want fewer of their goods, so producers supply fewer of them. 

Incentivising: higher prices increase the incentive to supply, because producers can make more profit. Lower prices decrease the incentive to supply, because producers make less profit.

Rationing: higher prices ration (or limit) goods to the consumers who will pay the most.

items.answer:
As you consume more of a good, the marginal utility from each extra unit will decrease.

E.g. as you eat more cupcakes, the marginal benefit of the next cupcake goes down each time as you become less hungry
The additional benefit received from consuming an extra unit of a good.

items.answer: The difference between what consumers are willing to pay and what they actually pay.
The difference between what producers are willing to sell for and what they actually sell for.

definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/30 - Negative externalities.html

items.answer: A cost to a producer or consumer inside the price mechanism.
Costs which affect third parties outside the price mechanism.
Where MSC = MSB and society's welfare is maximised.

definitions.title: public goods defs1
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/31 - public goods defs1.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/31 - public goods defs1.html

items.answer:
Public goods are non-excludable (you cannot stop others from using a public good) and non-rival (you can use the good at the same time as someone else).

E.g. A streetlight. You can't stop someone using the light from your streetlight so it's non-excludable. And several people can use the light from the street light at the same time so it's non-rival.

Public goods (e.g. streetlights) are non-excludable so others can use your public good for free, without paying.

Therefore, consumers won’t demand a public good, because they can just wait for someone else to buy and will use it for free.

Producers will not supply a public good because consumers will just use it for free, so producers cannot make a profit.

So, public goods will be underprovided by the market.
When the government provides a good (e.g. roads, education, streetlights)

definitions.title: Info gaps
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/32 - Info gaps.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/32 - Info gaps.html

items.answer: When consumers or producers lack the information needed to make an informed decision.
When someone doesn’t have full information about the benefits or costs of their decisions.

Asymmetric information is when one party knows more than another party in a transaction.

E.g. a used car seller knowing more about the car they're selling than the consumer; or an antique collector knowing more about the antique they're buying than the seller.

definitions.title: Types of economy
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/33 - Types of economy.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/33 - Types of economy.html

items.answer:
An economy where all resources are allocated by the price mechanism. There's NO government intervention.

E.g. Hong Kong in 1960s is a close example

An economy where all resources are allocated by the government. 

E.g. Cuba or North Korea in the 1980s

An economy where some resources are allocated by the price mechanism, and some are allocated by the government. 

E.g. the UK

definitions.title: Economic thinkers
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/34 - Economic thinkers.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/34 - Economic thinkers.html

items.answer:
Smith described the price mechanism as an "invisible hand". 

He argued that free markets were very efficient and that the government should only intervene in cases of market failure (e.g. externalities).

Marx argued that free markets would lead to capitalism and inequality. 

Capital owners would make huge profits, but workers would be paid low wages.

So Marx supported command economies, where a government can fairly allocate resources between everyone.

Friedrich Hayek described the price mechanism as a "communication network".

Changing prices told producers what consumers wanted and what they didn’t want, eliminating excess supply and demand. 

Hayek criticised Marx's ideas for a command economy. Hayek argued that a government would not have enough information to work out how to allocate resources effectively.

definitions.title: Definitions 1
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/35 - Definitions 1.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/35 - Definitions 1.html

items.answer:
Government intervention can have negative unintended consequences.

E.g. a tax on cigarettes can create a black market where cigarettes are sold without tax.

The miscellaneous costs of government intervention.

E.g. paperwork, legal fees, secretaries, managers.

When the government lacks the information needed to intervene most efficiently.

E.g. the government doesn’t know what size to set for its carbon tax.

Government intervention can distort the price mechanism.

E.g. a minimum price will create excess supply between Qd and Qs:

E.g. a maximum price will create excess demand between Qs and Qd:

definitions.title: Group 2
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/36 - Group 2.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/36 - Group 2.html

items.answer:
Consumers are influenced by the behaviour of others.

E.g. consumers may start smoking just because they see friends and celebrities smoking.

When consumers are in the habit of making certain decisions.

E.g. consumers may continue to buy car insurance from their current provider out of habit, even if there are cheaper car insurance providers available.

Consumers are bad at making calculations, estimating probabilities and working out future benefits/costs.

E.g. consumers may miscalculate which mobile phone contract offers the best value for money.

E.g. consumers overestimate the probability of a plane crash (it’s more likely that you will die wrestling a vending machine than in a plane crash).

E.g. consumers overestimate the future health benefits of “superfoods” and underestimate the future health costs of smoking.

definitions.title: Group 2
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/37 - Group 2.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/37 - Group 2.html

items.answer:
Permits which allow firms to pollute up to a certain limit. These permits can then be traded between firms.

E.g. Europe’s Emissions Trading Scheme (ETS). The EU government set a cap of 2bn tonnes of CO2, and issued permits to firms. It held the last 10% of permits back for auction.

The lowest price a good can be legally sold for.

E.g. Scotland’s minimum price on alcohol is 50p per unit of alcohol.

Note: a minimum price must be set above the market price otherwise it will have no effect.

When the government makes changes to the law to address market failure.

E.g. the Firearms Act which made it illegal to buy a handgun in the UK.

definitions.title: Group 1
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/38 - Group 1.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/38 - Group 1.html

items.answer: A benefit to a consumer inside the price mechanism.
Benefits to third parties outside the price mechanism.

items.prompt: Positive consumption externalities (or external benefits of consumption)
items.answer:
When the consumption of a good creates benefits to third parties outside the price mechanism.

E.g. consuming education will make you more productive and therefore also help your future employers.

items.prompt: Positive production externalities (or external benefits of production)
items.answer:
When the production of a good creates benefits to third parties outside the price mechanism.

E.g. producing honey requires bees to be raised; these bees will pollinate nearby crops benefitting farmers.

definitions.title: Group 2
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/39 - Group 2.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/39 - Group 2.html

items.prompt: Subsidy to internalise a positive consumption externality
items.answer: To correct a positive consumption externality, a subsidy must be set = the size of the external benefit between MSC and MPC at Qs.

The highest price a good can be legally sold for.

E.g. New York’s rent controls which set a maximum price on the rent a landlord can charge a tenant for a flat.

Note: a maximum price must be set below the market price otherwise it will have no effect.

definitions.title: Group 3
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/40 - Group 3.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/40 - Group 3.html

items.prompt: Negative consumption externalities (or external costs of consumption)
items.answer:
When the consumption of a good creates costs to third parties outside the price mechanism.

E.g. consuming cigarettes will damage the health of others via passive smoking.

items.prompt: Negative production externalities (or external costs of production)
items.answer:
When the production of a good creates costs to third parties outside the price mechanism.

E.g. a factory producing nuclear bombs will pollute nearby rivers with radiation.

MSC is above MPC because we have to add on the external costs.

Note: this diagram is only for negative production externalities – do not use it when discussing negative consumption externalities (the Principal Examiner has specifically warned against this!).

items.prompt: Tax to internalise a negative production externality
items.answer: To correct a negative production externality, a tax must be set = the size of the external cost between MSC and MPC at Qs.

Lower prices → stop underconsumption of goods with incomplete information (e.g. not knowing full health benefits of milk so under-consuming)

definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/44 - Subsidies essay plan.html

definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/45 - NASBIT.html

items.answer:
Factor:Ask yourself: 
Necessity or LuxuryDo we need it?
Addiction & HabitIs it addictive?
Are consumers in the habit of buying?

How close are the substitutes?
BrandingHow strong is the branding?

TimeMore time to search for substitutes in long run than short run

definitions.title: TEASS
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/46 - TEASS.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/46 - TEASS.html

items.answer:
Factor:Ask yourself:
TimeHow long does it take to produce?
Supply is more elastic in long run
State of EconomyRecession or recovery?
UK unemployment is 5-6%
Availability of FactorsHow easy is it to find the factors needed?
Can we import from abroad?
Spare CapacityDo firms have spare resources?
Stockpiles & PerishabilityIs it perishable?

definitions.title: OQT
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/47 - OQT.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/47 - OQT.html

items.answer:
Factor:Ask yourself: 
Opposite externalitiesWhat opposite externalities are there?

E.g. if negative externality is pollution from car manufacturer, evaluate with positive externality from jobs created.
QuantifyIt’s hard to quantify the externalities because of:
Missing information
Lots of of people being affected
Value judgements needed (e.g. how to quantify cost of noise pollution, totally normative)

TimeAre these effects short run (SR) or long run (LR)? Temporary or permanent?

definitions.title: State Provision essay plan
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/48 - State Provision essay plan.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/48 - State Provision essay plan.html

items.prompt: Go-to points and evaluations for state provision
items.answer:
Point Evaluation
Solves the free-rider problemOpportunity cost: state provision is expensive and means the government can’t spend in other areas; worsening its budget deficit.

State provision stops underconsumption of goods (which might occur because of external benefits or information gaps)Admin costs: government is not a profit-maximiser → no profit incentive → so it will let its admin costs (like paperwork) rise too high
Lower, fairer prices → improves consumer surplus and equality

definitions.title: Tradable Pollution Permits essay plan
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/49 - Tradable Pollution Permits essay plan.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/49 - Tradable Pollution Permits essay plan.html

items.prompt: Go-to points and evaluations for Tradable Pollution Permits
items.answer:
PointEvaluation
Pollution reduced efficiently → firms with lowest cost of reducing pollution will reduce most and then sell permits on to those with higher costs of reducing → pollution is kept within target, at the lowest cost to firms possibleBuying permits increases the cost of production → reduces firms’ supply of goods → higher prices for consumers
Tradable permits give firms incentive to invest in eco-friendly tech, so they can reduce pollution more and sell their permits for profitDifficult to quantity the amount of permits the government should supply

E.g. might oversupply permits, permits become too cheap, pollution isn’t controlled

E.g. might undersupply permits, permits become too scarce and expensive, pollution reduced too much → job losses
Auctions and fines raise revenue for governmentAdmin costs of monitoring firms’ pollution and running the auctions might be excessive

definitions.id: 50 - Pollution Permits vs Carbon Tax essay plan
definitions.title: Pollution Permits vs Carbon Tax essay plan
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/50 - Pollution Permits vs Carbon Tax essay plan.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/50 - Pollution Permits vs Carbon Tax essay plan.html

items.prompt: Go-to points for Pollution Permits vs Carbon Tax
items.answer:
Permits are more effective than carbon tax because...Carbon tax is more effective than permits because...
Permits can be traded → firms with lowest cost of reducing pollution → will reduce the most → sell permits on to those with higher costs of reducing pollution → pollution is reduced at the lowest cost Pollution permits use auctions to raise revenue → sometimes permits don’t sell at auction → so little revenue raised → taxes reliable and raises more revenue
Permits set a cap on pollution → whereas a carbon tax lets firms pollute as much as wanted → if firm can afford the taxTax provides certainty → firms know how much the tax will be → plan ahead → make investments. BUT firms don’t know how much the permits will cost in future → creates uncertainty → reduces investment

definitions.title: Substitutes (UBER) essay plan
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/51 - Substitutes (UBER) essay plan.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/14 - Microeconomics Theme 1/Definitions/51 - Substitutes (UBER) essay plan.html

items.answer:
Closeness of substitutes: We can argue that the two goods may not be close substitutes. 

E.g. iPhones and Samsungs may not be close substitutes because they have different features and apps.

definitions.title: Defination 1: Income, Wealth, Assets
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/01 - Defination 1 Income, Wealth, Assets.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/01 - Defination 1 Income, Wealth, Assets.html

items.answer: Income is the flow of money received by a person or economy each year.
Wealth is the sum, or stock, of a person or economy’s assets.
Items that you own.

definitions.title: Definitions 2: Circular Flow Equation
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/02 - Definitions 2 Circular Flow Equation.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/02 - Definitions 2 Circular Flow Equation.html

items.prompt: What is the Circular Flow Equation
items.answer: National income = national expenditure = national output

items.prompt: Within the Circular Flow of Income what is meant by Factor Incomes?
items.answer: Money paid by firms to households in return for factors of production.

definitions.title: Definitions 3: Real GDP
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/03 - Definitions 3 Real GDP.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/03 - Definitions 3 Real GDP.html

items.answer: Real GDP stands for Real Gross Domestic Product. Real GDP measures the value of an economy’s national output, adjusted for inflation: the total value of the goods and services the economy produces in a year, adjusted for inflation.

definitions.title: Definitions 4: Withdrawals/Leakages
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/04 - Definitions 4 Withdrawals Leakages.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/04 - Definitions 4 Withdrawals Leakages.html

items.prompt: What are leakages/withdrawals within the Circular Flow of Income?
items.answer: A leakage (also known as a withdrawal) is when money leaks out of the circular flow.

items.prompt: What are the different types of leakages/withdrawals from the circular flow of income?
items.answer:
1. Savings
  2. Imports
  3. Taxes

You can remember them as S I T

items.prompt: What are savings within the Circular Flow of Income?
items.answer: Disposable income that is not spent by households. Savings are a leakage.

items.prompt: What are imports within the Circular Flow of Income?
items.answer: Spending on foreign goods and services. Imports are a leakage.

items.prompt: What are taxes within the Circular Flow of Income?
items.answer: Money paid to the government by consumers, firms and workers. Taxes are a leakage.

definitions.title: Definitions 5: Injections
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/05 - Definitions 5 Injections.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/05 - Definitions 5 Injections.html

items.prompt: What are injections within the Circular Flow of Income?
items.answer: An injection is when money is injected into the circular flow.

items.prompt: What are the different types of injections into the circular flow of income?
items.answer:
1. Government spending
  2. Investment
  3. EXports

You can remember them as G I X

items.prompt: What is government spending within the Circular Flow of Income?
items.answer: When the government injects money into the economy by spending on schools, teachers, hospitals, doctors etc.

items.prompt: What are Exports within the Circular Flow of Income?
items.answer: When firms export goods abroad, foreign consumers pay us, so money is injected into our economy.

items.prompt: What is investment within the Circular Flow of Income?
items.answer: Banks use our savings to invest back into our firms, injecting money into the circular flow.

definitions.title: Definitions 6: Circular Flow Model
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/06 - Definitions 6 Circular Flow Model.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/06 - Definitions 6 Circular Flow Model.html

items.answer: Firms buy factors of production, like labour and land, from households. In return, they pay households factor incomes, like wages and rent. Households then spend their factor incomes on firms in exchange for the goods and services!

definitions.title: Definitions 1: Aggregate Demand, Demand
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/07 - Definitions 1 Aggregate Demand, Demand.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/07 - Definitions 1 Aggregate Demand, Demand.html

items.answer: Downward sloping line on a graph with Price Level on the Y-axis and Real GDP on the X-axis
Downward sloping line on a graph with Price on the Y-axis and Quantity on the X-axis

items.prompt: When should you draw a Demand curve, and when should you draw an Aggregate Demand curve?
items.answer:
Draw a demand curve (with price and quantity on the axes) when you’re referring to just one specific good (like beats headphones, oil or copper).

Draw an aggregate demand curve (with price level and Real GDP) when you’re referring to an entire economy.

definitions.title: Definitions 2: Aggregate Demand Formula
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/08 - Definitions 2 Aggregate Demand Formula.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/08 - Definitions 2 Aggregate Demand Formula.html

items.prompt: What is the aggregate demand formula and what do the terms mean?
items.answer:
Aggregate Demand = C + I + G + (X - M)

C = Consumption

I = Investment

G = Government Spending

(X - M) = Exports - Imports

definitions.title: Definitions 3: Percentage of Each Component
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/09 - Definitions 3 Percentage of Each Component.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/09 - Definitions 3 Percentage of Each Component.html

items.prompt: What is the share of each component of Aggregate Demand?
items.answer:
Consumption = 60%

Investment = 14%

Government Spending = 25%

Exports minus Imports = 1%

definitions.title: Definitions 4: Gross vs. Net Investment
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/10 - Definitions 4 Gross vs. Net Investment.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/10 - Definitions 4 Gross vs. Net Investment.html

items.answer: Net investment = Gross investment - Depreciation

definitions.id: 11 - Definitions 5 Contractions and Extensions in AD
definitions.title: Definitions 5: Contractions and Extensions in AD
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/11 - Definitions 5 Contractions and Extensions in AD.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/11 - Definitions 5 Contractions and Extensions in AD.html

items.answer: An increase in the price level leads to a decrease in real GDP.
A decrease in the price level leads to an increase in real GDP.

definitions.title: Definitions 1: Multiplier Effect
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/12 - Definitions 1 Multiplier Effect.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/12 - Definitions 1 Multiplier Effect.html

items.answer: The multiplier effect is when an initial increase in an injection leads to a much bigger, overall effect on the economy.
Income minus taxes.

definitions.id: 13 - Definitions 2 Multiplier Ratio, MPS, MPM, MPT, MPC, MPW
definitions.title: Definitions 2: Multiplier Ratio, MPS, MPM, MPT, MPC, MPW
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/13 - Definitions 2 Multiplier Ratio, MPS, MPM, MPT, MPC, MPW.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/13 - Definitions 2 Multiplier Ratio, MPS, MPM, MPT, MPC, MPW.html

items.answer:
The multiplier ratio shows the increase in GDP following an initial injection into the economy.

There are three ways to calculate the multiplier ratio: 

= 1/(1-MPC)

= 1/MPW

= 1/(MPS + MPT + MPM)
The proportion of additional income that is saved.
The proportion of additional income that is spent on imports.
The proportion of additional income that is taxed.

The proportion of additional income that is spent on goods in the economy (consumed).

MPC = 1 - MPW

The proportion of additional income that is withdrawn from the economy (saved, taxed or spent on imports). 

MPW = MPS + MPT + MPM

MPW = 1 - MPC

definitions.title: Definitions 3: Interest Rates
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/14 - Definitions 3 Interest Rates.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/14 - Definitions 3 Interest Rates.html

items.answer: The percentage of your savings paid to you by the bank.
The percentage of the amount you borrow that you need to pay to the bank.

items.prompt: How do changes in the interest rate affect saving, borrowing, and consumption?
items.answer:
SavingBorrowingConsumption
Increase in Interest RateIncreaseDecreaseDecrease
Decrease in Interest RateDecreaseIncreaseIncrease
The downward multiplier effect is where an initial increase in withdrawals leads to a larger decrease in aggregate demand.

definitions.title: Definitions 1: Exchange Rates
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/15 - Definitions 1 Exchange Rates.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/15 - Definitions 1 Exchange Rates.html

items.answer: An exchange rate tells us the price of one currency in terms of another.

definitions.title: Definitions 2: Currency Supply and Demand
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/16 - Definitions 2 Currency Supply and Demand.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/16 - Definitions 2 Currency Supply and Demand.html

items.prompt: What are the determinants of currency supply?
items.answer: Supply of a currency is determined by speculation, tourism abroad and imports.
Demand for a currency is determined by speculation, domestic tourism and exports.

definitions.title: Definitions 3: Appreciation, Depreciation
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/17 - Definitions 3 Appreciation, Depreciation.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/17 - Definitions 3 Appreciation, Depreciation.html

items.answer: The value of the currency increases (gets stronger).
The value of the currency decreases (gets weaker).

items.prompt: What is the effect of appreciation on imports and exports?
items.answer: Strong pound means imports get cheaper and exports get more expensive (SPICEE).

items.prompt: What is the effect of depreciation on imports and exports?
items.answer: Weak pound means imports get more expensive and exports get cheaper.

definitions.title: Definitions 4: Animal Spirits, Wealth Effects
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/18 - Definitions 4 Animal Spirits, Wealth Effects.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/18 - Definitions 4 Animal Spirits, Wealth Effects.html

items.answer: Animal Spirits refer to investor confidence, when animal spirits are high investor confidence is high.
When changes in consumers’ wealth affect their confidence and spending.

definitions.title: Definitions 5: Savings Ratio
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/19 - Definitions 5 Savings Ratio.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/19 - Definitions 5 Savings Ratio.html

items.answer:
The savings ratio gives the percentage of a consumer’s disposable income that is saved.

Savings ratio = (Total Savings/Disposable Income) x 100

definitions.id: 20 - Definitions 1 AS, Contractions and Extensions in AS
definitions.title: Definitions 1: AS, Contractions and Extensions in AS
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/20 - Definitions 1 AS, Contractions and Extensions in AS.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/20 - Definitions 1 AS, Contractions and Extensions in AS.html

items.answer: Total amount produced in an economy.
A contraction in aggregate supply refers to a decrease in the price level leading to a decrease in real GDP
An extension in aggregate supply refers to an increase in the price level leading to an increase in real GDP

definitions.id: 21 - Definitions 2 Spare capacity, bottleneck, full employment
definitions.title: Definitions 2: Spare capacity, bottleneck, full employment
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/21 - Definitions 2 Spare capacity, bottleneck, full employment.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/21 - Definitions 2 Spare capacity, bottleneck, full employment.html

items.prompt: Within the context of Keynesian LRAS what is spare capacity?
items.answer: When the economy is producing below its maximum potential output (horizontal part of Keynesian LRAS).

items.prompt: Within the context of Keynesian LRAS what is a bottleneck?
items.answer: When the economy is nearly producing at its maximum potential output (bendy part of Keynesian LRAS).

items.prompt: Within the context of Keynesian LRAS what is full employment?
items.answer: When the economy is producing at its maximum potential output (vertical part of Keynesian LRAS).

definitions.title: Definitions 1: Shifts in SRAS
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/22 - Definitions 1 Shifts in SRAS.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/22 - Definitions 1 Shifts in SRAS.html

items.prompt: What causes the SRAS curve to shift?
items.answer: The SRAS will shift if there is a change in the cost of production in an economy.

definitions.id: 23 - Definitions 1 Derived demand, Productivity, Capital Costs, Migration
definitions.title: Definitions 1: Derived demand, Productivity, Capital Costs, Migration
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/23 - Definitions 1 Derived demand, Productivity, Capital Costs, Migration.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/23 - Definitions 1 Derived demand, Productivity, Capital Costs, Migration.html

items.answer:
Derived demand is demand for a factor of production (like labour) that is derived from the demand of another good/service.

E.g. Demand for builders is derived from demand for houses

items.prompt: How does productivity affect labour demand?
items.answer:
Higher productivity → firms demand more workers because they’re more productive → can increase output and profit.

However → firms might demand fewer workers because now they don’t need as many workers to produce all output.

items.prompt: Explain the link between capital costs and labour. What’s the consequence of a decrease in cost of capital?
items.answer:
Capital and labour are substitutes.

Cost of capital decreases → cheaper to buy capital → firms will decrease demand for labour 

 E.g. As capital costs have decreased over the years, supermarkets have invested in Self-Service checkouts and reduced demand for till workers.

items.prompt: How does migration affect labour supply?
items.answer:
Workers migrate out of a country → country’s labour supply falls

Workers migrate into a country → country’s labour supply increases

definitions.title: Definitions 2: Benefits, Income Tax
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/24 - Definitions 2 Benefits, Income Tax.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/24 - Definitions 2 Benefits, Income Tax.html

items.prompt: Explain the consequence on labour supply of decreasing and of increasing benefits
items.answer:
Decreasing benefits → forces people into work → increases labour supply

Increasing benefits → people to quit their jobs to get benefits/stay unemployed → decreases labour supply

items.prompt: What happens to labour supply when income tax increases?
items.answer:
A higher income tax reduces the income workers keep, reducing incentive to work, decreasing supply.

But some workers need to earn a certain amount of income (e.g. to cover their mortgages for instance), so a higher tax means they’ll have to work more (to earn the same income after the tax change), increasing supply!

items.prompt: What happens to labour supply when income tax decreases?
items.answer:
A lower income tax increases the income workers keep, increasing incentive to work, increasing supply.

But some workers just need to earn a certain amount of income (to cover their mortgages), so a lower tax means they’ll be able to work less (to earn the same income after the tax change), decreasing supply!

definitions.title: Definitions 3: Non-Pecuniary Benefits, Education & Training
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/25 - Definitions 3 Non-Pecuniary Benefits, Education & Training.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/25 - Definitions 3 Non-Pecuniary Benefits, Education & Training.html

items.prompt: What is meant by Non-Pecuniary Benefits and how do they affect labour supply?
items.answer:
Non-pecuniary benefits are benefits of a job other than the wage received.

Like a company car or job satisfaction! 

If non-pecuniary benefits are higher, the job becomes more appealing so supply will shift out to the right. 

While if non-pecuniary benefits are lower, the job becomes less appealing so supply will shift in, to the left.

items.prompt: How does an increase in education and training affect labour supply?
items.answer: More training/education → more supply of skilled labour

items.prompt: How does an increase in education and training affect labour demand?
items.answer:
Demand for labour might increase because new training courses will increase the productivity of workers. This mean workers will be able to produce more goodsand make firms more profit. So firms will demand more labour.

However, if workers become more productive and each unit of labour can produce more output, firms might actually reduce demand for labour because they now need fewer workers to produce the same output as before!

definitions.title: Definitions 1: inflation, hyperinflation
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/26 - Definitions 1 inflation, hyperinflation.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/26 - Definitions 1 inflation, hyperinflation.html

items.answer: Inflation is a sustained increase in the general price level.
Refers to very high and increasing inflation, typically it has to be at least above 50%

definitions.title: Definitions 2: Deflation
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/27 - Definitions 2 Deflation.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/27 - Definitions 2 Deflation.html

items.answer: Deflation is a sustained fall in the general price level.

definitions.title: Definitions 3: Disinflation
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/28 - Definitions 3 Disinflation.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/28 - Definitions 3 Disinflation.html

items.answer: Disinflation is when the inflation rate is positive but decreasing.

definitions.id: 29 - Definitions Real GDP per capita, Remittances, Gross National Income
definitions.title: Definitions: Real GDP per capita, Remittances, Gross National Income
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/29 - Definitions Real GDP per capita, Remittances, Gross National Income.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/29 - Definitions Real GDP per capita, Remittances, Gross National Income.html

items.answer: The total output produced in an economy in a year divided by the population.
Money earned by workers which is sent home to family in another country.
Gross Domestic Product plus net income from abroad.

definitions.title: Definitions: Purchasing Power Parity
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/30 - Definitions Purchasing Power Parity.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/30 - Definitions Purchasing Power Parity.html

items.answer: A purchasing power parity (PPP) between two countries tells us how much of one currency is needed to purchase a basket of goods compared to another currency.

definitions.id: 31 - Definitions Actual Economic Growth, Potential Economic Growth
definitions.title: Definitions: Actual Economic Growth, Potential Economic Growth
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/31 - Definitions Actual Economic Growth, Potential Economic Growth.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/31 - Definitions Actual Economic Growth, Potential Economic Growth.html

items.answer: An actual increase in real GDP.

items.prompt: What is meant by an increase in Potential Economic Growth and how would this be shown on a diagram?
items.answer: An increase in the full employment potential output level, shown by a right shift of the LRAS.

definitions.title: Definitions: Underemployment
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/32 - Definitions Underemployment.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/32 - Definitions Underemployment.html

items.answer: Underemployment is when workers are employed but working fewer hours than they want to or underusing their skills.

definitions.id: 33 - Definitions Working Age Population, Active Population, Inactive Population
definitions.title: Definitions: Working Age Population, Active Population, Inactive Population
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/33 - Definitions Working Age Population, Active Population, Inactive Population.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/33 - Definitions Working Age Population, Active Population, Inactive Population.html

items.answer: People between the ages of 16 and 64.
People between the ages of 16 and 64 who are actively working or actively seeking work. Also called the workforce.
People between the ages of 16 and 64 who are neither actively working nor actively seeking work. This includes those who are unable to work.

definitions.id: 34 - Definitions Unemployment, unemployment level, unemployment rate, formula
definitions.title: Definitions: Unemployment, unemployment level, unemployment rate, formula
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/34 - Definitions Unemployment, unemployment level, unemployment rate, formula.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/34 - Definitions Unemployment, unemployment level, unemployment rate, formula.html

items.answer: When somebody is able to work and actively seeking work but is not working.
The number of people who are unemployed.
The number of unemployed people as a percentage of the economically active population.

items.prompt: What is the formula for the unemployment rate?
items.answer: Unemployment rate = (Number of unemployed people/economically active population) x 100

definitions.title: Definitions: Activity Rate
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/35 - Definitions Activity Rate.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/35 - Definitions Activity Rate.html

items.answer: The activity rate (or participation rate) is the number of economically active people as a percentage of the working age population.

items.prompt: What is the formula for the activity rate? (or participation rate)
items.answer: Activity Rate = (Number of Economically Active People/Working Age Population) x 100

definitions.title: Definitions: Employment Level, Employment Rate
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/36 - Definitions Employment Level, Employment Rate.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/36 - Definitions Employment Level, Employment Rate.html

items.answer: The number of people who are employed.
The number of employed people as a percentage of the working age population.

items.prompt: What is the formula for the employment rate?
items.answer: Employment rate = (Number of employed people/working age population) x 100

definitions.title: Definitions: Occupational and Geographical Immobility
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/37 - Definitions Occupational and Geographical Immobility.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/37 - Definitions Occupational and Geographical Immobility.html

items.answer: When workers can’t move between different jobs because they lack the skills.
When workers can’t move between different jobs because they can’t move to a different location.

definitions.title: Definitions: Types of Unemployment
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/38 - Definitions Types of Unemployment.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/38 - Definitions Types of Unemployment.html

items.answer: Real wage (or classical) unemployment occurs when wages are above the equilibrium wage creating an excess supply of labour.
Cyclical (or demand deficient) unemployment occurs when there is low aggregate demand in an economy and so firms reduce their derived demand for labour.
Structural unemployment occurs when the structure of the economy changes, shifting jobs from one sector or location to another and leaving immobile workers unemployed.
Frictional unemployment occurs when people are temporarily unemployed while searching for a new job.
Seasonal unemployment occurs when people are unemployed during certain seasons.

definitions.title: Definitions: Nominal GDP
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/39 - Definitions Nominal GDP.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/39 - Definitions Nominal GDP.html

items.answer: The total price of all goods and services produced in an economy

definitions.id: 40 - Definitions Balance of Payments, Current A C
definitions.title: Definitions: Balance of Payments, Current A/C
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/40 - Definitions Balance of Payments, Current A C.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/40 - Definitions Balance of Payments, Current A C.html

items.answer: The Balance of Payments is a record of payments between one country and the rest of the world.

items.prompt: What is the current account and what does it measure?
items.answer: The Current Account is part of the Balance of Payments. It measures trade in goods, trade in services, investment income and current transfers.

definitions.id: 41 - Definitions Current A C Deficit, Surplus, Capital & Financial A C
definitions.title: Definitions: Current A/C Deficit, Surplus, Capital & Financial A/C
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/41 - Definitions Current A C Deficit, Surplus, Capital & Financial A C.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/41 - Definitions Current A C Deficit, Surplus, Capital & Financial A C.html

items.answer: The Current Account is in deficit when outflows are greater than inflows and so it is a negative number.
The Current Account is in surplus when inflows are greater than outflows and so it is a positive number.

items.prompt: What is the Capital & Financial Account and what does it measure?
items.answer: The Capital & Financial Account is part of the Balance of Payments and tracks investments into, and out of, a country.

definitions.id: 42 - Definitions Claimant Count, Labour Force Survey
definitions.title: Definitions: Claimant Count, Labour Force Survey
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/42 - Definitions Claimant Count, Labour Force Survey.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/42 - Definitions Claimant Count, Labour Force Survey.html

items.answer: A survey to measure unemployment conducted by the International Labour Organisation.
A measure of unemployment which calculates the number of people claiming unemployment benefits.

definitions.id: 43 - Definitions Direct Tax, Indirect Tax, Income Tax, Corporation Tax
definitions.title: Definitions: Direct Tax, Indirect Tax, Income Tax, Corporation Tax
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/43 - Definitions Direct Tax, Indirect Tax, Income Tax, Corporation Tax.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/43 - Definitions Direct Tax, Indirect Tax, Income Tax, Corporation Tax.html

items.answer: A tax paid directly to the government such as corporation tax or income tax.
A tax paid by a producer when goods and services are purchased.
A tax which is paid directly by workers as a percentage of their income.
A tax which is paid directly by firms as a percentage of their profits.

definitions.id: 44 - Definitions Direct Tax, Indirect Tax, Income Tax, Corporation Tax
definitions.title: Definitions: Direct Tax, Indirect Tax, Income Tax, Corporation Tax
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/44 - Definitions Direct Tax, Indirect Tax, Income Tax, Corporation Tax.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/44 - Definitions Direct Tax, Indirect Tax, Income Tax, Corporation Tax.html

definitions.id: 45 - Definitions Direct Tax, Indirect Tax, Income Tax, Corporation Tax
definitions.title: Definitions: Direct Tax, Indirect Tax, Income Tax, Corporation Tax
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/45 - Definitions Direct Tax, Indirect Tax, Income Tax, Corporation Tax.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/45 - Definitions Direct Tax, Indirect Tax, Income Tax, Corporation Tax.html

definitions.id: 46 - Definitions Specific Tax, Ad Valorem Tax
definitions.title: Definitions: Specific Tax, Ad Valorem Tax
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/46 - Definitions Specific Tax, Ad Valorem Tax.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/46 - Definitions Specific Tax, Ad Valorem Tax.html

items.answer: A fixed amount of tax paid on each unit sold.
A tax charged as a percentage of the price of a good such as VAT.

definitions.id: 47 - Definitions Budget Deficit, Budget Surplus, Balanced Budget
definitions.title: Definitions: Budget Deficit, Budget Surplus, Balanced Budget
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/47 - Definitions Budget Deficit, Budget Surplus, Balanced Budget.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/47 - Definitions Budget Deficit, Budget Surplus, Balanced Budget.html

items.answer: When government spending is more than tax revenue.
When government spending is less than tax revenue.
When government spending is equal to tax revenue.

definitions.title: Definitions: National Debt, Government Bond
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/48 - Definitions National Debt, Government Bond.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/48 - Definitions National Debt, Government Bond.html

items.answer: Total debt built up by government borrowing over time.
A method that the government uses to borrow money. They sell a bond to an investor and pay it back at a later date with interest.

definitions.title: Definitions: Inequality
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/49 - Definitions Inequality.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/49 - Definitions Inequality.html

items.answer: The difference in living standards between the rich and poor.

definitions.title: Definitions: Recession, Boom, Recovery, Slump
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/50 - Definitions Recession, Boom, Recovery, Slump.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/50 - Definitions Recession, Boom, Recovery, Slump.html

items.answer: When the economy experiences negative economic growth for two consecutive quarters. Also the part of the business cycle where real GDP is at its lowest.
The part of the business cycle where real GDP is at its highest.
The part of the business cycle where real GDP is decreasing.
The part of the business cycle where real GDP is increasing.

definitions.id: 51 - Definitions Potential Trend GDP, Negative Output Gap, Positive Output Gap
definitions.title: Definitions: Potential Trend GDP, Negative Output Gap, Positive Output Gap
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/51 - Definitions Potential Trend GDP, Negative Output Gap, Positive Output Gap.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/51 - Definitions Potential Trend GDP, Negative Output Gap, Positive Output Gap.html

items.answer: The sustainable rate of GDP growth caused by improvements in productive capacity overtime.
When actual GDP is below potential trend GDP.
When actual GDP is above potential trend GDP.

definitions.title: Definitions: Supply-side, Demand-side
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/52 - Definitions Supply-side, Demand-side.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/52 - Definitions Supply-side, Demand-side.html

items.answer: Government policies which aim to influence aggregate supply.
Government policies which aim to influence aggregate demand.

definitions.id: 53 - Definitions Fiscal Policy, Contractionary Fiscal Policy, Expansionary Fiscal Policy
definitions.title: Definitions: Fiscal Policy, Contractionary Fiscal Policy, Expansionary Fiscal Policy
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/53 - Definitions Fiscal Policy, Contractionary Fiscal Policy, Expansionary Fiscal Policy.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/53 - Definitions Fiscal Policy, Contractionary Fiscal Policy, Expansionary Fiscal Policy.html

items.answer: When the government uses tax and government spending to influence the economy.
When the government increases tax and decreases government spending in order to decrease aggregate demand.
When the government decreases tax and increases government spending in order to increases aggregate demand.

definitions.title: Definitions: Benefits Trap
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/54 - Definitions Benefits Trap.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/54 - Definitions Benefits Trap.html

items.answer:
When benefits get too high and unemployed workers are actually better 

off staying unemployed and claiming benefits than working.

definitions.title: Definitions: Infrastructure, Crowding Out
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/55 - Definitions Infrastructure, Crowding Out.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/55 - Definitions Infrastructure, Crowding Out.html

items.answer: Items needed for businesses to operate such as roads and telecommunications networks.
When government spending increases, the government demands more borrowed money, land, labour and capital. The price and interest rate increases which increases costs for firms and reduces supply.

definitions.id: 56 - Definitions Monetary Policy, Base Interest Rate, Expansionary Monetary Policy, Contractionary Monetary Policy
definitions.title: Definitions: Monetary Policy, Base Interest Rate, Expansionary Monetary Policy, Contractionary Monetary Policy
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/56 - Definitions Monetary Policy, Base Interest Rate, Expansionary Monetary Policy, Contractionary Monetary Policy.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/56 - Definitions Monetary Policy, Base Interest Rate, Expansionary Monetary Policy, Contractionary Monetary Policy.html

items.answer: When the central bank changes the base interest rate or the money supply in order to influence aggregate demand within an economy.
The interest rate, set by the Bank of England, showing the rate at which they will lend money to highstreet banks.
Decreasing the base interest rate or increasing the money supply in order to increase aggregate demand and increase the rate of inflation.
Increasing the base interest rate or decreasing the money supply in order to decrease aggregate demand and decrease the rate of inflation.

definitions.title: Definitions: Quantitative Easing
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/57 - Definitions Quantitative Easing.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/57 - Definitions Quantitative Easing.html

items.answer: When the central bank purchases financial assets from banks, increasing the banks’ supply of money and increasing lending to consumers and firms. This increases consumption and investment, shifting out AD.

definitions.title: Definitions 1: Interventionist and Market Based
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/58 - Definitions 1 Interventionist and Market Based.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 12/15 - Macroeconomics Theme 2/Definitions/58 - Definitions 1 Interventionist and Market Based.html

items.answer: Policies where the government is actively involved in increasing aggregate supply.
Policies where the government aims to increase aggregate supply by decreasing intervention in the economy and allowing the market to operate efficiently.

definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/01 - AC and TFC and AC curves.html

definitions.title: Definitions 3 (MR-TR relationship, MR-TR curves)
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/02 - Definitions 3 (MR-TR relationship, MR-TR curves).json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/02 - Definitions 3 (MR-TR relationship, MR-TR curves).html

items.prompt: Marginal Revenue (MR) and Total Revenue (TR) relationship
items.answer:
When MR is positive, TR will increase as quantity increases.

When MR is negative, TR will decrease as quantity increases.

When MR is positive, TR will increase as quantity increases.

When MR is negative, TR will decrease as quantity increases.

So the TR curve is increasing when MR is positive and the TR curve is decreasing when MR is negative.

definitions.title: Definitions 3 (MR-TR relationship, MR-TR curves)
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/03 - Definitions 3 (MR-TR relationship, MR-TR curves).json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/03 - Definitions 3 (MR-TR relationship, MR-TR curves).html

definitions.id: 04 - Definitions 1 (SR, LR, Fixed, Variable, TC = TFC + TVC)
definitions.title: Definitions 1 (SR, LR, Fixed, Variable, TC = TFC + TVC)
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/04 - Definitions 1 (SR, LR, Fixed, Variable, TC = TFC + TVC).json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/04 - Definitions 1 (SR, LR, Fixed, Variable, TC = TFC + TVC).html

items.answer: The short run is when at least one factor of production is fixed.
The long run is when all factors are variable.
Costs which do not vary with output e.g. rent and salaries
Costs which vary with output e.g. raw materials and wages

Total cost = Total Variable Cost + Total Fixed Cost

TC = TVC + TFC

definitions.title: Definitions 2 (MR, MR curve)
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/05 - Definitions 2 (MR, MR curve).json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/05 - Definitions 2 (MR, MR curve).html

items.answer:
MR = ∆TR/∆Q

Marginal revenue is the additional revenue from selling one extra unit.

The MR curve must:

  1. Start at the same point as AR  
  2. Cross the Q axis at half the quantity AR crosses at
  3. MR should end at the same quantity that AR ends at

An important fact about the Marginal Revenue (MR) curve

As price decreases and quantity increases, MR decreases.

MR decreases from positive to negative.

definitions.id: 06 - Definitions 2 (AC and TFC and AC curves)
definitions.title: Definitions 2 (AC and TFC and AC curves)
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/06 - Definitions 2 (AC and TFC and AC curves).json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/06 - Definitions 2 (AC and TFC and AC curves).html

items.answer: TFC does not vary with output because fixed costs are fixed!

definitions.id: 07 - Definitions 1 (AR, TR, AR curve, AR = price)
definitions.title: Definitions 1 (AR, TR, AR curve, AR = price)
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/07 - Definitions 1 (AR, TR, AR curve, AR = price).json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/07 - Definitions 1 (AR, TR, AR curve, AR = price).html

items.answer:
Total Revenue = Price x Quantity

TR = P x Q

Total revenue is total amount of money a firm receives from its sales.

AR = TR/Q

If we simplify this formula, we find that Average Revenue = Price (AR = P)
Remember: when drawing an AR curve, you must ensure your curve starts from the price axis.

definitions.id: 08 - Definitions 4 changing elasticity along demand curve
definitions.title: Definitions 4: changing elasticity along demand curve
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/08 - Definitions 4 changing elasticity along demand curve.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/08 - Definitions 4 changing elasticity along demand curve.html

items.prompt: PED changes along the demand curve
items.answer:
At high prices, demand is elastic because a % change in price will have a big impact, so consumers will be very responsive.

E.g. 10% of £1000 = £100 

At low prices, demand is inelastic because a % change in price will have a small impact, so consumers will be unresponsive.

E.g. 10% of £10 = £1

When MR is positive, demand will be elastic.

When MR is 0, demand will be unitary elastic.  

When MR is negative, demand will be inelastic.

definitions.title: Definitions 5: revenue maximisation
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/09 - Definitions 5 revenue maximisation.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/09 - Definitions 5 revenue maximisation.html

items.answer: A firm’s total revenue is maximised when MR = 0 (no more revenue can be gained at this point).
A firm’s total revenue is maximised when MR = 0, at quantity Q1, so the price is P1.

definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/10 - Definitions 3 MC words formula, diminishing marginal returns, MC curve, MC curve explanation.html

items.answer:
Marginal cost is the cost of selling an additional unit.

MC = ∆TC/∆Q

items.prompt: Diminishing marginal returns (or the law of diminishing marginal returns)
items.answer: In the short run, as more factors are employed, the marginal returns from these factors will eventually decrease!

Remember: it looks like a Nike tick.

Explaining why the marginal cost (MC) curve goes down and then up

MC decreases because initially workers will specialise, increase productivity and decreasing marginal cost.

MC will then increase because diminishing marginal returns will set in, which will decrease productivity and increase marginal cost.

definitions.title: Definitions 5: Internal economies of scale
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/11 - Definitions 5 Internal economies of scale.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/11 - Definitions 5 Internal economies of scale.html

items.answer: Internal economies of scale are when long run average costs fall as a firm’s quantity increases.

items.prompt: Types of internal economies of scale (RMFPTM)
items.answer:
Richard’s Mum Flies Past The Moon

Risk-bearing economies 

Managerial economies 

Financial economies 

Purchasing economies  

Technical economies 

Marketing economies

Bigger firms can use their big profits to diversify into new areas, reducing the cost of failure in one sector.

E.g. Virgin have diversified into 400 different areas.

Bigger firms can afford to hire highly skilled specialist managers, increasing a firm’s productivity and decreasing their LR average costs!

E.g. Amazon hire specialist accounting, software and marketing managers.

Bigger firms are less risky, so they can secure cheaper loans, reducing their long run average costs.

E.g. Alibaba.com borrowed £3bn at a tiny 2% interest rate.

Bigger firms can bulk-buy and negotiate lower prices, reducing their long run average costs!

E.g. McDonald’s purchase thousands of tonnes of chicken breast at a very low average cost.

Bigger firms can invest in specialist capital, to increase a firm’s productivity and decrease their long run average costs.

E.g. Amazon’s warehouse robots and Kameoka’s robot lettuce farmers have massively increased productivity.

Bigger firms can spread their marketing costs across many units, decreasing their long run average costs.

E.g. Guinness, Beats or Nike, who spend millions on marketing in total but just pennies on average, because their costs are so spread out.

definitions.title: Definitions 7: External economies of scale
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/12 - Definitions 7 External economies of scale.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/12 - Definitions 7 External economies of scale.html

items.answer: External economies of scale will reduce long run average cost, as the industry expands.
External economies of scale will shift the entire long run average cost curve downwards, from LRAC to LRAC1

items.prompt: External economies of scale: lower recruitment costs
items.answer:
When an industry expands, lots of specialist workers will be move to that area to find work. This makes it easier to recruit workers, reducing a firm’s recruitment costs, decreasing their LRAC.

E.g. In Silicon valley, there are 60,000+ coders which reduces tech firms’ recruitment costs.

items.prompt: External economies of scale: knowledge transfers
items.answer:
When an industry expands, knowledge will be transferred between firms. This helps firms learn more effective new production techniques, decreasing their LRAC.  

E.g. In LA’s film industry, the green screen technique was spread by knowledge transfer. Using green screens has reduced film producers’ long run average costs.

definitions.title: Definitions 6: Internal diseconomies of scale
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/13 - Definitions 6 Internal diseconomies of scale.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/13 - Definitions 6 Internal diseconomies of scale.html

items.answer: Internal diseconomies of scale lead to a rise in long run average cost, as a firm expands.

items.prompt: Types of internal diseconomies of scale (ABC)
items.answer:
Alienation

Bureaucracy  

Communication

Workers feel alienated in very large firms, like they’re just another cog in the machine. This leads to demotivation, decreasing productivity, increase LRAC.

E.g. large call centres in India.
Bureaucracy is all the paperwork, managers, filing and secretaries that a firm has to pay for when it expands, increasing LRAC.
In big firms, employees may argue with each other and communication will be slow because big firms have so many layers. These factors will reduce productivity, increasing LRAC.

definitions.title: Definitions 4: MC and AVC relationship, MC and AVC graph, AVC formula

items.answer: AVC = TVC/Q

When MC is below AVC, AVC will decrease

When MC is above AVC, AVC will increase 

When MC = AVC, AVC is at its lowest
