definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/15 - 1.html

items.answer: When a firm is producing at its lowest average cost, where MC = AC.
When welfare is maximised, where MC = AR.
When a firm is producing above its average cost curve for a given level of output.
Dynamic efficiency is how changing technology improves a firm's output potential over time.

definitions.title: Definitions 1: influencers of the firm
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/16 - Definitions 1 influencers of the firm.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/16 - Definitions 1 influencers of the firm.html

items.answer: The firm is influenced by its: owners, shareholders, directors/managers, workers and consumers.
Shareholders usually look to maximise profit.

Directors and managers usually look to maximise sales or revenue.  

Maximising sales increases their sales bonus and maximising revenue increases company size, boosting their prestige.
Workers want higher wages, job security and improved working conditions.
Consumers want lower prices, better customer service and quality, and they also care about social and environmental causes (e.g. homeless people and polar bears).

definitions.title: Definitions 2: business objectives
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/17 - Definitions 2 business objectives.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/17 - Definitions 2 business objectives.html

items.answer: Where MR = 0.
When a firm maximises its sales without making a loss, where AC = AR.
Profit satisficing is when a company makes enough profit to satisfy its influencers, but then pursues other objectives (social, environmental, personal).

definitions.title: Definitions 1: types of profit
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/18 - Definitions 1 types of profit.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/18 - Definitions 1 types of profit.html

items.answer: In economics, Total Cost doesn’t just include actual physical costs (e.g. raw materials, wages), it also includes opportunity cost (e.g. the opportunity cost of the manager’s time).
Normal profit is when TR = TC. The firm will just cover its opportunity cost, so it will stay in the market.
Losses are when TR is less than TC. The firm will make less than normal profit, so it will leave the market because it can’t cover its opportunity cost.

items.prompt: Supernormal profit (also known as abnormal profit)
items.answer: Supernormal profit is when TR is greater than TC. The firm will make more than normal profit.

definitions.id: 19 - Definitions 1 why some firms grow while others stay small
definitions.title: Definitions 1: why some firms grow while others stay small
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/19 - Definitions 1 why some firms grow while others stay small.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/19 - Definitions 1 why some firms grow while others stay small.html

items.prompt: Reasons why some firms grow while others stay small
items.answer:
Reason to growReason to stay small (Evaluation)
Increase sales and profitHowever, firms might lack the finance to expand 
More market power, so firms can increase prices and increase profitHowever, regulations might prevent firms from growing
Diversify and enjoy risk-bearing economies e.g. Apple launching new products, iPod, iPhone, iPadHowever, firm might be in a niche market or selling personalised goods
Exploit internal economies of scale to decrease LRAC and increase profitHowever, firm might run into internal diseconomies of scale
Owners objective might be to run a hugely successful company e.g. Steve Jobs wanting to change the world However, other owners might just want a quiet life running a small firm

definitions.id: 20 - Definitions 2 divorce of ownership and control, principal-agent problem
definitions.title: Definitions 2: divorce of ownership and control, principal-agent problem
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/20 - Definitions 2 divorce of ownership and control, principal-agent problem.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/20 - Definitions 2 divorce of ownership and control, principal-agent problem.html

items.prompt: The divorce of ownership and control
items.answer: When the managers/directors of a firm are different from the owners of the firm.

The principal-agent problem is when the agent (e.g. the manager who controls the business) pursues different objectives to the principal (e.g. the shareholders who own the business).

E.g. managers (agent) look to sales maximise for sales bonuses while shareholders (principal) look to profit maximise.
Private sector firms are firms owned by private individuals.

definitions.id: 21 - Definitions 3 public sector firms, private sector firms, for-profit firms, not-for-profit firms
definitions.title: Definitions 3: public sector firms, private sector firms, for-profit firms, not-for-profit firms
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/21 - Definitions 3 public sector firms, private sector firms, for-profit firms, not-for-profit firms.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/21 - Definitions 3 public sector firms, private sector firms, for-profit firms, not-for-profit firms.html

items.answer: Public sector firms are owned by the government.
For-profit firms are looking to make a profit.

Not-for-profit firms are not looking to just make a profit, they also pursue other social and environmental objectives.

E.g. charities like Oxfam and Young Enterprise.

definitions.title: Definitions 4: organic growth, inorganic growth
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/22 - Definitions 4 organic growth, inorganic growth.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/22 - Definitions 4 organic growth, inorganic growth.html

items.answer:
Organic growth is when a firm grows by investing in itself to increase output.

E.g. reinvesting profits, selling shares, taking a bank loan.

Inorganic growth is when a firm grows by merging with, or acquiring another firm.

E.g. when Google acquired Youtube.

definitions.id: 23 - Definitions 5 vertical integration, backward vertical integration, forward vertical integration, horizontal integration, conglomerate
definitions.title: Definitions 5: vertical integration, backward vertical integration, forward vertical integration, horizontal integration, conglomerate
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/23 - Definitions 5 vertical integration, backward vertical integration, forward vertical integration, horizontal integration, conglomerate.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/23 - Definitions 5 vertical integration, backward vertical integration, forward vertical integration, horizontal integration, conglomerate.html

items.answer: Vertical integration is when firms at different stages of the same production process join together.

Backwards vertical integration is when a firm integrates backwards, with a firm further away from the consumer.

E.g. Ford’s car factory integrating with the tyre manufacturer.

Forward vertical integration is when a firm integrates forwards, with a firm who is closer to the consumer.

E.g. Ford’s car factory integrating with a car showroom.

Horizontal integration which is when firms at the same stage of the production process join together.  

E.g. T-Mobile and Orange merging to form EE.

Conglomerate integration is when two firms in unrelated industries join together.  

E.g. when Pepsi acquired Quaker Oats.

definitions.id: 24 - Definitions 6 pros and cons of organic growth
definitions.title: Definitions 6: pros and cons of organic growth
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/24 - Definitions 6 pros and cons of organic growth.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/24 - Definitions 6 pros and cons of organic growth.html

items.prompt: Pros and cons of organic growth
items.answer:
ProsCons
Firstly, using a bank loan or reinvesting profit to grow organically, a firm’s owner will keep ownership and control over the company, and take most of the profit.However, if the owner grows organically by selling lots of shares, they will lose ownership to the shareholders.

Or if the owner sets up lots of franchises, they will lose control to their franchise managers. 
Secondly, organic growth is low risk because the firm expands by increasing its own output.

Inorganic growth is higher risk because a firm might move into a completely new market.		However, organic growth can also mean slower growth, compared to inorganic where mergers/acquisitions speed up growth.

definitions.id: 25 - Definitions 7 pros and cons of vertical integration
definitions.title: Definitions 7: pros and cons of vertical integration
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/25 - Definitions 7 pros and cons of vertical integration.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/25 - Definitions 7 pros and cons of vertical integration.html

items.prompt: Pros and cons of vertical integration
items.answer:
ProsCons
Firstly, vertical integration enables firms to control the supply chain.

E.g. if an electricity-producer buys a power grid, it can prevent competitor firms from entering the market, by refusing to let them use the power grid.

So the electricity producer will take over the market, increasing sales and profit.		However, vertical integration could lead to regulation if it prevents competition.

Firms may be banned from vertically integrating or forced to pay penalties as high as 10% of annual turnover. This will increase firms’ costs, reducing profit. 
Secondly, vertical integration can cut out intermediary costs (or middleman costs) - like transport costs and markups. This will reduce a firm’s costs and increasing profits.However, costs may increase because of diseconomies of scale and acquisition costs - like when eBay bought PayPal for $1.5bn. 
Thirdly, vertical integration improves access to consumers and raw materials.

Vertically integrating forward helps receive customer feedback directly, so a firm can improve its product, increase sales and increase profit.

Vertically integrated backward means a firm can control quality, improving its final product’s quality, increasing sales and profit.		However, vertical integration can backfire because of a lack of expertise. If a firm integrates into a stage of the production process it knows nothing about, it won’t know how to organise production, decreasing efficiency, increasing costs, decreasing profits.

definitions.id: 26 - Definitions 8 pros and cons of horizontal integration
definitions.title: Definitions 8: pros and cons of horizontal integration
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/26 - Definitions 8 pros and cons of horizontal integration.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/26 - Definitions 8 pros and cons of horizontal integration.html

items.prompt: Pros and cons of horizontal integration
items.answer:
ProsCons
Firstly, horizontal mergers can lead to economies of scale which reduce a firm’s LRAC and increasing profit.However, firms can also suffer from diseconomies of scale, increasing a firm’s LRAC, decreasing their profit.  
Secondly, horizontal integration can lead to rationalisation: when firms reorganise to avoid duplicated costs.
E.g. after two firms merge, only one accounting team will be needed, not two		However, rationalisation can lead to job losses because duplicated departments will be fired.
Thirdly, horizontal integration reduces wasteful competition because the two firms are now working together.However, horizontal integration can lead to brand dilution if the firm's’ brands are very different.
E.g. if Dom Perignon merged with ASDA Sparkling Water

definitions.id: 27 - Definitions 11 pros and cons of demergers to workers, pros and cons of demerger to consumers
definitions.title: Definitions 11: pros and cons of demergers to workers, pros and cons of demerger to consumers
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/27 - Definitions 11 pros and cons of demergers to workers, pros and cons of demerger to consumers.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/27 - Definitions 11 pros and cons of demergers to workers, pros and cons of demerger to consumers.html

items.prompt: Pros and cons of demergers to workers
items.answer:
ProsCons
Workers will benefit from reduced cultural conflicts between different divisions with different attitudes. This will reduce tension, increase productivity and job satisfaction, benefitting workers.

However, workers will suffer from lower job security. They won’t know which new division they’ll join after the demerger and if one of the division is sold to raise funds, workers may lose their jobs entirely.

items.prompt: Pros and cons of demerger to consumers
items.answer:
ProsCons
Consumers will benefit after a demerger as each new smaller firm can specialise in their job, increasing efficiency, reducing costs and prices, but also increasing quality. However, if the demerged companies are too small, it might reduce economies of scale, increasing LRAC, so firms will have to put their prices up.

definitions.id: 28 - Definitions 10 demerger, 4 reasons for business demergers
definitions.title: Definitions 10: demerger, 4 reasons for business demergers
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/28 - Definitions 10 demerger, 4 reasons for business demergers.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/28 - Definitions 10 demerger, 4 reasons for business demergers.html

items.answer:
1. Demergers reduce diseconomies of scale.

2. Demergers enable each new separate firm to specialise. 

3. A firm can sell one of its demerged divisions and its assets to raise funds. 

4. Demergers reduce conflicts between different cultures within a firm.

definitions.title: Definitions 1: N-Firm Concentration Ratios
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/29 - Definitions 1 N-Firm Concentration Ratios.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/29 - Definitions 1 N-Firm Concentration Ratios.html

items.answer:
An N-firm concentration ratio measures how much market share the N largest firms in a market have.

E.g. a 3 -firm concentration ratio measures how much market share the 3 largest firms in a market have.

definitions.title: Definitions 1: monopoly, three assumptions
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/30 - Definitions 1 monopoly, three assumptions.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/30 - Definitions 1 monopoly, three assumptions.html

items.answer:
When there’s only one dominant firm in a market. Legally, a monopoly is a firm with over 25% market share.

E.g. Microsoft, who owned 90% of the operating system market.

When modelling a monopoly, we assume:

  1. There’s only one firm in the market.  
  2. The monopoly will want to profit maximise .
  3. High barriers to entry .

definitions.id: 31 - Definitions 2 incumbent firm, legal barriers, sunk costs, sunk costs as a barrier to entry, economies of scale, brand loyalty, anti-competit
definitions.title: Definitions 2: incumbent firm, legal barriers, sunk costs, sunk costs as a barrier to entry, economies of scale, brand loyalty, anti-competitive practices
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/31 - Definitions 2 incumbent firm, legal barriers, sunk costs, sunk costs as a barrier to entry, economies of scale, brand loyalty, anti-competit.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/31 - Definitions 2 incumbent firm, legal barriers, sunk costs, sunk costs as a barrier to entry, economies of scale, brand loyalty, anti-competit.html

items.answer: A firm currently in the market.
Legal barriers include patents, trademarks and copyright. They enable incumbent firms to legally prevent new firms from stealing their ideas and entering their market.

Costs that cannot be recovered.

E.g. advertising - once you’ve paid for a TV ad, you can’t get that money back.
High sunk deter new firms from entering because firms know that if they fail, they won’t be able to recover any of their sunk costs.
Economies of scale mean incumbent firms can keep their costs and prices low, creating a barrier to entry because smaller new firms without economies of scale can’t compete on price.
Strong branding from incumbent firms makes it hard for new entrants, with weaker branding, to make any sales.

Anti-competitive (or restrictive) practices include anything a firm might do, to restrict competition.

E.g. vertical integration: firms can vertically integrate to take control of scarce resources (like the power grid); and then refuse to let new firms use these scarce resources, stopping them from entering the market.

definitions.title: Definitions 4: Natural monopoly
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/33 - Definitions 4 Natural monopoly.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/33 - Definitions 4 Natural monopoly.html

items.answer: A natural monopoly is when it’s naturally most efficient if only one firm is in the market.

items.prompt: Reasons for natural monopolies: high sunk costs
items.answer:
Very high sunk costs mean it would be inefficient if a second firm entered the market and also had to incur those sunk costs - so one firm is most efficient.

E.g. TFL’ sunk costs have been estimated as high as £129bn - it would completely inefficient for a second firm to waste £129bn to enter the market, too.

items.prompt: Reasons for natural monopolies: huge economies of scale
items.answer: Huge economies of scale means it’s most efficient for just one firm to be in the market so it can increases its output massively to reach its MES.

definitions.title: Definitions 5: Constant marginal cost simplification
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/34 - Definitions 5 Constant marginal cost simplification.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/34 - Definitions 5 Constant marginal cost simplification.html

items.answer: When MC is constant, MC will be a straight line and MC = AC.

definitions.title: Definitions 6: price discrimination, 3 conditions
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/35 - Definitions 6 price discrimination, 3 conditions.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/35 - Definitions 6 price discrimination, 3 conditions.html

items.answer:
Price discrimination is when a firm charges different groups of consumers different prices, for the same good.

E.g. students get lower prices for train tickets than adults.
The firm must have market power: it must be able to change their prices

items.prompt: Price discrimination condition 2: information on elasticities
items.answer: The firm must be able to identify which consumers are elastic and which are inelastic.

The firm must be able to limit reselling: it must be able to limit elastic consumers from selling cheap tickets to inelastic consumers

E.g. trains require student IDs or student Oysters, to stop inelastic adult consumers using resold student tickets!

definitions.title: Definitions 7: price discrimination diagrams
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/36 - Definitions 7 price discrimination diagrams.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/36 - Definitions 7 price discrimination diagrams.html

items.answer:
Elastic demand is flatter. 

Inelastic demand is steeper. 

And total demand juts out at lower prices, because elastic consumers join the market.

definitions.title: Definitions 1: Perfect competition
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/37 - Definitions 1 Perfect competition.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/37 - Definitions 1 Perfect competition.html

items.answer:
A perfectly competitive market has:

  1. Many small buyers and sellers
  2. No barriers to entry or exit
  3. Homogeneous products
  4. Perfect information

definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/38 - Definitions 1 monopolistic competition.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/38 - Definitions 1 monopolistic competition.html

items.answer:
• There are many small buyers and sellers
  • Low barriers to entry or exit
  • Differentiated goods

definitions.id: 39 - Definitions 1 contestable market, contestability, hit & run competition
definitions.title: Definitions 1: contestable market, contestability, hit & run competition
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/39 - Definitions 1 contestable market, contestability, hit & run competition.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/39 - Definitions 1 contestable market, contestability, hit & run competition.html

items.answer: A market with low barriers to entry/exit.

How easy it is to enter a market.

E.g. the airlines market has low contestability because it’s tough to enter. 

E.g. the mango market has high contestability because it’s easy to enter.

In contestable markets, if an incumbent firm is making supernormal profit in the short run, new firms will enter (or “hit”) industry.

They’ll undercut the incumbent firm to steal away its consumers and make supernormal profit.  

To get rid of the new entrants, the incumbent firm has to set price = AC so only normal profit can be made. New firm will then leave the market (“run”) because supernormal profit is gone.

definitions.title: Definitions 1: Oligopoly
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/40 - Definitions 1 Oligopoly.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/40 - Definitions 1 Oligopoly.html

items.answer:
Features of an oligopolistic market:

  1. The market is dominated by a few large sellers 
  2. High barriers to entry/exit
  3. Differentiated goods
  4. Interdependence between firms

definitions.id: 41 - Definitions 2 collusion, overt collusion, tacit collusion, collusion is illegal
definitions.title: Definitions 2: collusion, overt collusion, tacit collusion, collusion is illegal
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/41 - Definitions 2 collusion, overt collusion, tacit collusion, collusion is illegal.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/41 - Definitions 2 collusion, overt collusion, tacit collusion, collusion is illegal.html

items.answer: When two firms work together to limit competition (e.g. price-fixing).

When there’s a formal agreement between firms to limit competition .

E.g. a phone call/contract/handshake between firms.
When there’s an unspoken agreement between firms to limit competition.
Only overt collusion is illegal!

definitions.id: 42 - Definitions 3 price wars, predatory pricing, limit pricing
definitions.title: Definitions 3: price wars, predatory pricing, limit pricing
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/42 - Definitions 3 price wars, predatory pricing, limit pricing.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/42 - Definitions 3 price wars, predatory pricing, limit pricing.html

items.answer:
When firms undercut each other with lower prices to steal the other firms’ consumers.

E.g. baked bean wars, when supermarkets undercut each other and drove the price of baked beans down to just 3p

When a firm aggressively cuts its prices below AVC to force out competitors from the market.

Short run: firm incurs a loss 

Long run: firm forces out its competitors, so they can take over the market.

When an incumbent firm uses its economies of scale to set a price low enough to limit new firms from entering.

Small new firms, without economies of scale, won’t be able to compete so they’ll stay out of the market. 

Limit pricing is a barrier to entry.

definitions.title: Definitions 4: non-price competition
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/43 - Definitions 4 non-price competition.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/43 - Definitions 4 non-price competition.html

items.answer:
When firms compete without changing price, e.g:

  1. Advertising
  2. Loyalty cards
  3. Branding
  4. Quality

definitions.id: 44 - Definitions 2 Elasticity of labour supply factors
definitions.title: Definitions 2: Elasticity of labour supply factors
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/44 - Definitions 2 Elasticity of labour supply factors.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/44 - Definitions 2 Elasticity of labour supply factors.html

items.prompt: What are the three main factors that affect the elasticity of labour supply?
items.answer:
There are three main factors:

  1. Skills & Qualifications
  2. Unemployment
  3. Time

items.prompt: How do skills and qualifications affect the elasticity of labour supply?
items.answer:
In markets where workers require few skills or qualifications, supply will be elastic.

E.g Becoming a McDonald’s worker. If McDonald’s offers higher wages, it’ll be super easy for new workers to become McDonald’s employees - so quantity supplied will increase a lot, supply will be very responsive to changes in wage, suggesting elastic supply! 

In markets where workers require lots skills or qualifications, supply will be inelastic.

E.g Becoming a heart surgeon. If the NHS increased salaries for heart surgeons massively, potential new surgeons would need to go through 15 years of training, and develop incredible surgical skills! So in the short run at least, the number of heart surgeons really isn’t going to increase by much, suggesting unresponsive inelastic labour supply!

items.prompt: How does unemployment affect the elasticity of labour supply?
items.answer:
When unemployment is high, there are lots of people looking for work, so workers will be very responsive, suggesting labour supply is elastic.

When unemployment is low, there are very few people looking for work, so workers will be unresponsive, suggesting labour supply is inelastic.

items.prompt: How does time affect the elasticity of labour supply?
items.answer:
In the short run, without enough time to apply or train for jobs, workers won’t be able to respond much so supply of labour will be inelastic.

In the long run, there’ll be more time apply and train for jobs, so workers will be able to respond more so supply for labour will be elastic.

definitions.id: 45 - Definitions 1 Elasticity of labour demand factors
definitions.title: Definitions 1: Elasticity of labour demand factors
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/45 - Definitions 1 Elasticity of labour demand factors.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/45 - Definitions 1 Elasticity of labour demand factors.html

items.prompt: What are the three main factors that affect elasticity of labour demand?
items.answer:
There are three main factors:

  1. Substitutes
  2. % of Total Cost
  3. Time

items.prompt: How do substitutes affect the elasticity of labour demand?
items.answer:
If workers are easy to substitute/replace, demand will be elastic.

E.g. McDonald’s workers can easily be replaced by self-service checkouts, so if wages increase, McDonald’s will be responsive and switch to capital, suggesting elastic demand. 

If workers are hard to substitute/replace, demand will be inelastic.

E.g. Superstar footballers cannot easily be replaced, so if wages increase, football clubs have no alternatives so they can’t respond, suggesting inelastic demand.

items.prompt: How does % of total cost affect the elasticity of labour demand?
items.answer:
If wages make up a small % of total cost, any increase in wage will have a small impact, so demand for labour will be unresponsive and therefore inelastic.


If wages make up a large % of total cost, an increase in wages will have a large impact, so demand for labour will be responsive and therefore elastic.

items.prompt: How does time affect the elasticity of labour demand?
items.answer:
In the short run, without enough time to search for substitutes, firms won’t be able to respond much so demand for labour will be inelastic. Demand will be steeper - so wages will be higher.  


In the long run, there’ll be enough time to search for substitutes, firms will be able to respond more so demand for labour will be elastic. Demand will be flatter - so wages will be lower.

definitions.id: 46 - Definitions 9 pros and cons of conglomerate integration
definitions.title: Definitions 9: pros and cons of conglomerate integration
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/46 - Definitions 9 pros and cons of conglomerate integration.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/46 - Definitions 9 pros and cons of conglomerate integration.html

items.prompt: Pros and cons of conglomerate integration
items.answer:
ProsCons
Firstly, conglomerate integration can lead to risk-bearing economies. Integrating with an unrelated firm helps a firm diversify, reducing cost of failure in one sector. However, firms can also suffer from diseconomies of scale, increasing a firm’s LRAC, decreasing their profit.  
Secondly, conglomerate integration can increase brand awareness. Consumers of one firm will become aware of the other, increasing both firms’ sales.
E.g. when TATA and Starbucks merged, TATA consumers became aware of Starbucks		However, conglomerate integration can also lead to brand dilution, if one firm’s brand image negatively affects another.
E.g. Pepsi diluting Quaker Oats’ healthy porridge image. 
Thirdly, conglomerate integration can lead to knowledge transfers between firms which increases dynamic efficiency.
E.g. Apple and Beats innovating new headphones 		However, conglomerate integration can fail if firms lack expertise.
E.g. if Nando’s acquired with Ferrari, Nando’s wouldn’t know how to run Ferrari

definitions.title: Definitions
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/47 - Definitions.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/47 - Definitions.html

items.answer:
A monopsony is when there’s only one dominant buyer in the market

E.g. the NHS is the only dominant buyer of doctors in the UK

A minimum wage is the lowest wage a firm can legally employ a worker for. 

E.g. for over-25s in the UK, the national minimum wage is £7.50

A trade union is a group of workers who collectively bargain to improve employee welfare.  

E.g. the NUT, the National Union of Teachers, who have previously gone on strikes to bargain for higher pay from the government.

A maximum wage is the highest wage a worker can legally be employed for. 

E.g. Jeremy Corbyn has argued for a maximum wage set at 20x the lowest wage within a company.
Occupational immobility is when workers can’t move between different jobs (or occupations) because they lack the skills needed.
Geographical immobility is when workers struggle to move between different areas.

definitions.title: Definitions 1
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/48 - Definitions 1.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/16 - Microeconomics Theme 3/Definitions/48 - Definitions 1.html

items.answer:
Privatisation is the transfer of a business, industry, or service from public to private ownership and control.

E.g. British Telecom was sold to private investors in 1984.
