definitions.id: 01 - Definitions Absolute Advantage, Comparative Advantage, Theory of Comparative Advantage
definitions.title: Definitions: Absolute Advantage, Comparative Advantage, Theory of Comparative Advantage
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/01 - Definitions Absolute Advantage, Comparative Advantage, Theory of Comparative Advantage.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/01 - Definitions Absolute Advantage, Comparative Advantage, Theory of Comparative Advantage.html

items.answer: A country has a comparative advantage in the production of a good if they can produce it at a lower opportunity cost than another country
If countries specialise in the production of the goods in which they have a comparative advantage, global output will increase.

definitions.id: 02 - Assumptions and Limitations of the Theory of Comparative Advantage
definitions.title: Assumptions and Limitations of the Theory of Comparative Advantage
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/02 - Assumptions and Limitations of the Theory of Comparative Advantage.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/02 - Assumptions and Limitations of the Theory of Comparative Advantage.html

items.prompt: Assumptions of the Theory of Comparative Advantage
items.answer: The assumptions of the theory of comparative advantage are that average costs remain constant; there are no trade barriers and no transport costs.

items.prompt: Limitations of the Theory of Comparative Advantage
items.answer: The limitations of the theory of comparative advantage are that diseconomies of scale increase average costs; there are trade barriers such as tariffs and there are transport costs when trading.

definitions.title: Advantages and Disadvantages of Specialisation
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/03 - Advantages and Disadvantages of Specialisation.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/03 - Advantages and Disadvantages of Specialisation.html

items.answer:
1. Leads to an increase in global output and living standards
  2. May create economies of scale
  3. Can lead to lower prices and more choice for consumers

1. Benefits are based on unrealistic assumptions
  2. May lead to over dependence on imports and exports
  3. Can cause demotivation which will decrease productivity and increase prices.

definitions.id: 04 - Definitions 1 Globalisation, FDI, TNC, Characteristics of Globalisation
definitions.title: Definitions 1: Globalisation, FDI, TNC, Characteristics of Globalisation
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/04 - Definitions 1 Globalisation, FDI, TNC, Characteristics of Globalisation.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/04 - Definitions 1 Globalisation, FDI, TNC, Characteristics of Globalisation.html

items.answer: Increased integration of different economies around the world.
An investment made by a firm in one country into a firm in another country, to gain control over the foreign firm.
A transnational corporation is a company that operates in two or more countries.

1. An increase in international movement of labour
  2. An increase in international movement of financial capital
  3. An increase in specialisation
  4. An increase in international trade
  5. An increase in trade-to-GDP ratios.

definitions.id: 05 - Definitions 2 Containerisation, Trade Liberalisation, Tariff, Causes of Globalisation
definitions.title: Definitions 2: Containerisation, Trade Liberalisation, Tariff, Causes of Globalisation
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/05 - Definitions 2 Containerisation, Trade Liberalisation, Tariff, Causes of Globalisation.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/05 - Definitions 2 Containerisation, Trade Liberalisation, Tariff, Causes of Globalisation.html

items.answer: An efficient and relatively low cost system of transport that uses a common size of steel container to transport goods.
The reduction and removal of trade barriers.
A tax on imported goods

1. Improvements in transport
  2. Improvements in IT
  3. Containerisation
  4. Trade liberalisation

definitions.title: Definitions 3: Impact of Globalisation I
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/06 - Definitions 3 Impact of Globalisation I.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/06 - Definitions 3 Impact of Globalisation I.html

items.answer: A method of pricing goods and services transferred within TNCs in order to reduce the amount of corporation tax paid.

items.prompt: Impact of Globalisation on Individual Countries
items.answer: Globalisation has led to an increase in living standards but also an increase in overdependence.
Globalisation has led to an increase in tax revenue but also an increase in transfer pricing.
Globalisation has reduced costs through relocation but it has also increased barriers to entry.

definitions.title: Definitions 4: Impact of Globalisation II
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/07 - Definitions 4 Impact of Globalisation II.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/07 - Definitions 4 Impact of Globalisation II.html

items.answer: Globalisation has led to an increase consumer choice and lower prices but it has arguably reduced happiness.
Globalisation has led to an increase in international job opportunities but increased structural unemployment.

items.prompt: Impact of Globalisation on the Environment
items.answer: Globalisation has increased international cooperation to fight climate change but it has increased global warming.

definitions.id: 08 - Definitions 1 Improvement and Deterioration in Terms of Trade
definitions.title: Definitions 1: Improvement and Deterioration in Terms of Trade
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/08 - Definitions 1 Improvement and Deterioration in Terms of Trade.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/08 - Definitions 1 Improvement and Deterioration in Terms of Trade.html

items.answer: The terms of trade formula equals the index of export prices divided by the index of import prices, multiplied by 100.
An improvement in the terms of trade means that there has either been an increase in the index of export prices or a decrease in the index of import prices.
A deterioration in the terms of trade means that there has either been a decrease in the index of export prices or an increase in the index of import prices.

definitions.id: 09 - Definitions 2 Factors Influencing a Country’s Terms of Trade
definitions.title: Definitions 2: Factors Influencing a Country’s Terms of Trade
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/09 - Definitions 2 Factors Influencing a Country’s Terms of Trade.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/09 - Definitions 2 Factors Influencing a Country’s Terms of Trade.html

items.prompt: Impact of Raw Material Prices on Terms of Trade
items.answer: An increase in raw material prices will deteriorate the terms of trade for countries who import raw materials and improve the terms of trade for countries who export raw materials.

items.prompt: Impact of Tariffs on Terms of Trade
items.answer: An increase in tariffs will deteriorate the terms of trade and a decrease in tariffs will improve the terms of trade.

items.prompt: Impact of Exchange Rates on Terms of Trade
items.answer: An exchange rate depreciation will deteriorate the terms of trade and an appreciation will improve the terms of trade.

items.prompt: Impact of Inflation Rates on Terms of Trade
items.answer: A low inflation rate relative to trading partners will deteriorate the terms of trade and a high inflation rate relative to trading partners will improve the terms of trade.

definitions.id: 10 - Definitions 3 Impact of Changes in a Country’s Terms of Trade
definitions.title: Definitions 3: Impact of Changes in a Country’s Terms of Trade
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/10 - Definitions 3 Impact of Changes in a Country’s Terms of Trade.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/10 - Definitions 3 Impact of Changes in a Country’s Terms of Trade.html

items.prompt: Impact of Terms of Trade on Living Standards
items.answer: An improvement in the terms of trade will increase living standards and a deterioration in the terms of trade will decrease living standards.

items.prompt: Impact of Terms of Trade on Competitiveness
items.answer: An improvement in the terms of trade will decrease competitiveness and a deterioration in the terms of trade will increase competitiveness.

definitions.title: Definitions 1: Quotas, Tariffs, Subsidies
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/11 - Definitions 1 Quotas, Tariffs, Subsidies.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/11 - Definitions 1 Quotas, Tariffs, Subsidies.html

items.answer: A strict limit on the quantity of imports
Grants given to domestic producers by the government in order to encourage supply and reduce price. This will decrease demand for imports.

definitions.title: Definitions 2: Non-Tariff Barriers
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/12 - Definitions 2 Non-Tariff Barriers.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/12 - Definitions 2 Non-Tariff Barriers.html

items.answer: Restrict free trade by setting rules and regulations for imports to follow.
Health and safety regulations, environmental regulations and labelling.

definitions.id: 13 - Definitions 3 Reasons for Restrictions on Free Trade, Dumping, Infant Industry
definitions.title: Definitions 3: Reasons for Restrictions on Free Trade, Dumping, Infant Industry
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/13 - Definitions 3 Reasons for Restrictions on Free Trade, Dumping, Infant Industry.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/13 - Definitions 3 Reasons for Restrictions on Free Trade, Dumping, Infant Industry.html

items.prompt: Reasons for Restrictions on Free Trade
items.answer: A country may impose restrictions on free trade to; prevent dumping, protect domestic jobs, protect infant industries and improve health and safety standards.
Dumping is where foreign firms aggressively cut their prices, below average variable costs, to force out domestic producers from a market.
New industries which do not benefit from economies of scale and can’t compete with larger industries from other countries.

definitions.id: 14 - Definitions 4 Impact of Restrictions on Free Trade
definitions.title: Definitions 4: Impact of Restrictions on Free Trade
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/14 - Definitions 4 Impact of Restrictions on Free Trade.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/14 - Definitions 4 Impact of Restrictions on Free Trade.html

items.prompt: Impact of Protectionist Policy on Living Standards
items.answer: Trade barriers reduce world output and therefore reduce living standards.

items.prompt: Impact of Protectionist Policy on Consumers
items.answer: Trade barriers increase prices and restrict choice for consumers.

items.prompt: Impact of Protectionist Policy on Equality
items.answer: Trade barriers increase the price of necessities and therefore increase inequality.

items.prompt: Impact of Protectionist Policy on Producers
items.answer: Trade barriers create less incentive for domestic producers to become more efficient.

items.prompt: Impact of Protectionist Policy on Governments
items.answer: Trade barriers become difficult for governments to remove.

definitions.title: Definitions 1: Trading Blocs
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/15 - Definitions 1 Trading Blocs.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/15 - Definitions 1 Trading Blocs.html

items.answer: Trading blocs are formed when countries join together and all agree to remove trade barriers between each other.
The four types of trading bloc are; Free Trade Areas, Customs Unions, Common Markets, Monetary Unions.

definitions.title: Definitions 2: Types of Trading Blocs
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/16 - Definitions 2 Types of Trading Blocs.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/16 - Definitions 2 Types of Trading Blocs.html

items.answer: Trade barriers are removed between member countries.
Where members of a trading bloc must all have the same tariffs on external non-member countries.
Trade barriers are removed between member countries and there is a common external tariff placed on countries outside the trading bloc.
Trade barriers are removed between member countries and there is a common external tariff placed on countries outside the trading bloc and factors of production can move freely between countries.
Trade barriers are removed between member countries and there is a common external tariff placed on countries outside the trading bloc and countries share a common currency.

definitions.id: 17 - Definitions 3 The European Union, the European Single Market and the Eurozone
definitions.title: Definitions 3: The European Union, the European Single Market and the Eurozone
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/17 - Definitions 3 The European Union, the European Single Market and the Eurozone.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/17 - Definitions 3 The European Union, the European Single Market and the Eurozone.html

items.answer: The European Union is a customs union with 27 member countries. They have removed trade barriers between each other and share a common external tariff with non-EU countries.
The European Single Market is a common market, which means that factors of production, like labour, can move freely between the 31 countries.
The Eurozone is a monetary union meaning that all 20 of its members have adopted a common currency - the Euro.

definitions.id: 18 - Definitions 4 Trade Creation and Trade Diversion
definitions.title: Definitions 4: Trade Creation and Trade Diversion
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/18 - Definitions 4 Trade Creation and Trade Diversion.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/18 - Definitions 4 Trade Creation and Trade Diversion.html

items.answer: When the removal of tariffs means that the quantity of imports increases and trade is created.
When the creation of a trading bloc means that trade is diverted from low cost producers outside the bloc to high cost producers inside the bloc.

definitions.title: Definitions 1: The World Trade Organisation
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/19 - Definitions 1 The World Trade Organisation.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/19 - Definitions 1 The World Trade Organisation.html

items.answer: An organisation of 164 countries that regulates world trade.

items.prompt: Functions of the World Trade Organisation
items.answer: There are two functions; to organise rounds of talks to negotiate trade deals and to settle trade disputes.

definitions.id: 20 - Definitions 1 Factors Influencing the Patterns of Trade
definitions.title: Definitions 1: Factors Influencing the Patterns of Trade
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/20 - Definitions 1 Factors Influencing the Patterns of Trade.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/20 - Definitions 1 Factors Influencing the Patterns of Trade.html

items.prompt: Four Factors That Influence Patterns of Trade
items.answer: The four factors that influence the pattern of trade are; comparative advantage, emerging economies, trading blocs, exchange rates.

items.prompt: Impact of Comparative Advantage on Pattern of Trade
items.answer: If a country has a comparative advantage in a good, they will export more of it.

items.prompt: Impact of Emerging Economies on Pattern of Trade
items.answer: There has been an increase in exports and imports from the emerging economies; Brazil, Russia, India, China and South Africa.

items.prompt: Impact of Trading Blocs on Pattern of Trade
items.answer: Trading blocs can lead to trade creation and trade diversion.

items.prompt: Impact of Exchange Rates on Pattern of Trade
items.answer: An exchange rate appreciation will lead to an increase in imports and a decrease in exports.

definitions.id: 21 - Definitions 4 Foreign Direct Investment, Hot Money Flows
definitions.title: Definitions 4: Foreign Direct Investment, Hot Money Flows
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/21 - Definitions 4 Foreign Direct Investment, Hot Money Flows.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/21 - Definitions 4 Foreign Direct Investment, Hot Money Flows.html

items.answer: Investments made by a firm in one country into a firm in another country to gain control over the foreign firm.
Money that investors move internationally between banks to maximise the interest they receive.

definitions.id: 22 - Definitions 1 Balance of Payments, Components of Balance of Payments, Components of Current Account
definitions.title: Definitions 1: Balance of Payments, Components of Balance of Payments, Components of Current Account
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/22 - Definitions 1 Balance of Payments, Components of Balance of Payments, Components of Current Account.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/22 - Definitions 1 Balance of Payments, Components of Balance of Payments, Components of Current Account.html

items.answer: A record of payments between one country and the rest of the world.

items.prompt: What are the main components of the Balance of Payments?
items.answer: The Balance of Payments is made up of the Current Account and the Financial & Capital Account.

items.prompt: What are the components of the Current Account?
items.answer: The components of the Current Account are: trade in goods, trade in services, investment income and current transfers.

definitions.id: 23 - Definitions 2 Investment Income, Current Transfers, Remittances
definitions.title: Definitions 2: Investment Income, Current Transfers, Remittances
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/23 - Definitions 2 Investment Income, Current Transfers, Remittances.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/23 - Definitions 2 Investment Income, Current Transfers, Remittances.html

items.prompt: What component of the Balance of Payments does Investment Income fall under and what is it?
items.answer: A component of the Current Account which includes any rent or profit earned on an investment made abroad.

items.prompt: What component of the Balance of Payments do Current Transfers fall under and what are they?
items.answer: A component of the Current Account, which includes money that is transferred abroad without getting any goods or services back in exchange.
Money transferred back home from relatives working abroad is a form of current transfers.

definitions.id: 24 - Definitions 5 Factors Affecting the Current Account I
definitions.title: Definitions 5: Factors Affecting the Current Account I
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/24 - Definitions 5 Factors Affecting the Current Account I.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/24 - Definitions 5 Factors Affecting the Current Account I.html

items.prompt: How do Exchange Rates affect the current account?
items.answer: An appreciation of the exchange rate will decrease the current account or a depreciation of the exchange rate will increase the current account.

items.prompt: How do Relative Inflation Rates affect the current account?
items.answer: High relative inflation rates will decrease the current account or low relative inflation rates will increase the current account.

items.prompt: Explain the impact of low productivity on production costs and the current account
items.answer: Low productivity will increase production costs and decrease the current account.

definitions.id: 25 - Definitions 6 Factors Affecting the Current Account II
definitions.title: Definitions 6: Factors Affecting the Current Account II
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/25 - Definitions 6 Factors Affecting the Current Account II.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/25 - Definitions 6 Factors Affecting the Current Account II.html

items.prompt: Explain the impact that the quality of exports have on a country’s current account
items.answer: A decrease in quality will decrease the current account. An increase in quality will increase the current account.

items.prompt: Explain the impact that growth has on a country’s current account
items.answer: High economic growth will decrease the current account. Low levels of economic growth will put upward pressure on the current account.

items.prompt: Explain the impact of protectionism on the current account
items.answer: The removal of protectionist measures will decrease the current account or the implementation of protectionist measures will increase the current account.

definitions.id: 26 - Definitions 3 Current Account Deficit, Surplus, Equilibrium, Trade Balance
definitions.title: Definitions 3: Current Account Deficit, Surplus, Equilibrium, Trade Balance
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/26 - Definitions 3 Current Account Deficit, Surplus, Equilibrium, Trade Balance.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/26 - Definitions 3 Current Account Deficit, Surplus, Equilibrium, Trade Balance.html

items.answer: When total outflows from the current account are greater than total inflows.
When total inflows to the current account are greater than total outflows.
When total outflows from the current account are equal to total inflows.
Total value of exports minus total value of imports.

definitions.title: Definitions 7: Expenditure Reducing, Expenditure Switching
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/27 - Definitions 7 Expenditure Reducing, Expenditure Switching.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/27 - Definitions 7 Expenditure Reducing, Expenditure Switching.html

items.prompt: What are Expenditure Reducing Policies and how do they relate to the current account?
items.answer: Policies which aim to reduce expenditure on all goods in order to reduce import expenditure and improve the current account.
Policies which aim to switch expenditure from imported goods to domestic goods. They include trade barriers and low interest rates.

definitions.title: Definitions: Revaluation, Devaluation
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/28 - Definitions Revaluation, Devaluation.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/28 - Definitions Revaluation, Devaluation.html

items.answer: When a country raises their fixed exchange rate.
When a country lowers their fixed exchange rate.

definitions.title: Definitions: Fixed, Floating, Managed
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/29 - Definitions Fixed, Floating, Managed.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/29 - Definitions Fixed, Floating, Managed.html

items.answer: The government, or central bank, fix the value of one currency to the value of another.
The government, or central bank, will only intervene to keep their exchange rate within a certain range.
The government or central bank does not intervene at all. The exchange rate is simply determined by market forces.

definitions.id: 30 - Definitions Nominal and Real Exchange Rates
definitions.title: Definitions: Nominal and Real Exchange Rates
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/30 - Definitions Nominal and Real Exchange Rates.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/30 - Definitions Nominal and Real Exchange Rates.html

items.answer: The nominal exchange rate tells us the price of one currency in terms of another.
The real ER tells us how much a currency is worth relative to the prices of goods and services in another country.
Real Exchange Rate = (Nominal Exchange Rate x Domestic Price Level)/Foreign Price Level

definitions.title: Definitions: The J-Curve, Marshall-Lerner Condition
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/31 - Definitions The J-Curve, Marshall-Lerner Condition.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/31 - Definitions The J-Curve, Marshall-Lerner Condition.html

items.answer: Following a depreciation of the exchange rate, the J-curve effect shows a worsening of the Current Account in the short run and then an improvement in the long run.

Following a depreciation of the exchange rate, the Current Account will only improve if the sum of the elasticity of demand for exports and the elasticity of demand for imports is greater than one (i.e.) PEDx + PEDm > 1).

definitions.title: Definitions: Competitive Depreciation, Currency War
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/32 - Definitions Competitive Depreciation, Currency War.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/32 - Definitions Competitive Depreciation, Currency War.html

items.answer: When a country depreciates their own currency to keep their exports cheap and competitive.
A currency war is when countries depreciate their exchange rates to make their exports more competitive than other countries’.

definitions.id: 33 - Definitions International Competitiveness, Unit Labour Costs, Global Competitiveness
definitions.title: Definitions: International Competitiveness, Unit Labour Costs, Global Competitiveness
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/33 - Definitions International Competitiveness, Unit Labour Costs, Global Competitiveness.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/33 - Definitions International Competitiveness, Unit Labour Costs, Global Competitiveness.html

items.answer: A country is more competitive if their exports are sold at a lower price or a higher quality than other countries.
Unit labour cost is the cost of wages per unit of output produced.
Unit Labour Cost = Total Wage Cost/Total Output
An index which attempts to rate the international competitiveness of different countries based on a variety of factors.

definitions.title: Definitions 1: Absolute and Relative Poverty
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/34 - Definitions 1 Absolute and Relative Poverty.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/34 - Definitions 1 Absolute and Relative Poverty.html

items.answer: Absolute poverty is being unable to afford the basic necessities such as food, clean water and shelter.
The World Bank measures it by looking at the number of people living on less than $2.15 a day.
Relative poverty is defined as earning less than a particular percentage of a country’s average income.
In the UK, a person is in relative poverty if they earn less than 60% of the median UK income.

definitions.id: 35 - Definitions 1 Income Inequality, Lorenz Curve, Gini Coefficient, Formula
definitions.title: Definitions 1: Income Inequality, Lorenz Curve, Gini Coefficient, Formula
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/35 - Definitions 1 Income Inequality, Lorenz Curve, Gini Coefficient, Formula.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/35 - Definitions 1 Income Inequality, Lorenz Curve, Gini Coefficient, Formula.html

items.answer: When the best paid workers take home more income than the rest of a country’s workers.
A graph to show income inequality. The further a Lorenz curve is from the 45° line of perfect equality, the more unequal the economy is.
A measure of income inequality. The higher the number, the more unequal the economy is.
The formula is: G = A/(A+B)

definitions.id: 36 - Definitions 2 Wealth Inequality, Assortative Mating, r g hypothesis
definitions.title: Definitions 2: Wealth Inequality, Assortative Mating, r > g hypothesis
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/36 - Definitions 2 Wealth Inequality, Assortative Mating, r g hypothesis.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/36 - Definitions 2 Wealth Inequality, Assortative Mating, r g hypothesis.html

items.answer: When assets are not shared equally between individuals
When rich, successful people marry other rich, successful people.
If r, the rate of return on wealth, is greater than g, the rate of income growth, then wealth will grow faster than incomes.

definitions.title: Definitions 1: Economic Development, Indicators
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/37 - Definitions 1 Economic Development, Indicators.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/37 - Definitions 1 Economic Development, Indicators.html

items.answer: An increase in living standards in an economy.

items.prompt: What are the indicators of a more economically developed country?
items.answer: An economy may be more developed if there is greater access to clean water, more mobile phones, greater access to the internet, higher energy consumption and a lower proportion of people working in agriculture.

definitions.id: 38 - Definitions 2 Human Development Index, Advantages and Disadvantages
definitions.title: Definitions 2: Human Development Index, Advantages and Disadvantages
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/38 - Definitions 2 Human Development Index, Advantages and Disadvantages.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/38 - Definitions 2 Human Development Index, Advantages and Disadvantages.html

items.answer: It is a measure of development which includes education (average years of schooling), health (life expectancy) and living standards (real GNI per capita).

items.prompt: What are the main advantages of the Human Development Index?
items.answer: It is easy to compare development between countries and it is holistic as it uses a range of factors.

items.prompt: What are the main disadvantages of the Human Development Index?
items.answer: It ignores the distribution of income and would be more reliable if it used more factors.

definitions.title: Definitions 3: IHDI and MPI
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/39 - Definitions 3 IHDI and MPI.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/39 - Definitions 3 IHDI and MPI.html

items.prompt: What is the Inequality-Adjusted Human Development Index?
items.answer: The IHDI is the normal HDI adjusted to account for different levels of inequality. If the IHDI is below the HDI, then there is inequality.

items.prompt: What does the Multidimensional Poverty Index measure?
items.answer: Measures education, health and living standard using 10 different indicators. It measures the number of people in poverty and the average intensity of poverty.

definitions.title: Definitions 1: Human Capital
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/40 - Definitions 1 Human Capital.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/40 - Definitions 1 Human Capital.html

items.answer: When workers don’t have the necessary knowledge, skills or assets to be productive.

definitions.id: 41 - Definitions Savings Gaps, Microfinance, Harrod-Domar Model
definitions.title: Definitions: Savings Gaps, Microfinance, Harrod-Domar Model
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/41 - Definitions Savings Gaps, Microfinance, Harrod-Domar Model.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/41 - Definitions Savings Gaps, Microfinance, Harrod-Domar Model.html

items.answer: A gap between the amount of money held at banks in savings and the amount of money that firms want to borrow from banks.
Small loans provided to small businesses who otherwise would have no access to financial services
A model to explain how low savings make it difficult for firms to be able to invest which limits economic growth.

definitions.id: 42 - Definitions Property Rights, Dead Capital, Collateral
definitions.title: Definitions: Property Rights, Dead Capital, Collateral
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/42 - Definitions Property Rights, Dead Capital, Collateral.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/42 - Definitions Property Rights, Dead Capital, Collateral.html

items.answer: Property rights give people the legal right to their property and so they can go to court if their property is stolen
Possessions which lack property rights
An asset given as security for the repayment of a loan

definitions.id: 43 - Definitions Corruption, Fair Trade Scheme, Fair Trade Premium
definitions.title: Definitions: Corruption, Fair Trade Scheme, Fair Trade Premium
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/43 - Definitions Corruption, Fair Trade Scheme, Fair Trade Premium.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/43 - Definitions Corruption, Fair Trade Scheme, Fair Trade Premium.html

items.answer: When government officials use public funds for private gain.
When you buy fair trade products, part of the fair trade price you pay goes directly to the producers and so it increases their incomes.
A communal fund that fair trade farmers can spend on whatever will develop their communities the most.

definitions.title: Definitions: Landlocked
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/44 - Definitions Landlocked.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/44 - Definitions Landlocked.html

items.answer: When a country has no coasts as it is surrounded by other countries.
When you tell somebody that has borrowed money from you that they no longer have to pay it back.

definitions.title: Definitions: Infant Industry
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/45 - Definitions Infant Industry.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/45 - Definitions Infant Industry.html

items.answer: A young industry which does not yet produce enough output to benefit from economies of scale.
Where a government reduces the value of their fixed exchange rate in order to make exports more competitive.

definitions.title: Definitions: Primary Product, Price Instability
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/46 - Definitions Primary Product, Price Instability.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/46 - Definitions Primary Product, Price Instability.html

items.answer: A product made from raw materials.
Small changes in the supply and demand for primary products can lead to big changes in price.

definitions.title: Definitions: Buffer Stock Scheme
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/47 - Definitions Buffer Stock Scheme.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/47 - Definitions Buffer Stock Scheme.html

items.answer: A scheme where the government buys and sells primary products from a market in order to reduce price fluctuations.

definitions.id: 48 - Definitions The Prebisch-Singer Hypothesis, The Lewis Model, Industrialisation
definitions.title: Definitions: The Prebisch-Singer Hypothesis, The Lewis Model, Industrialisation
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/48 - Definitions The Prebisch-Singer Hypothesis, The Lewis Model, Industrialisation.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/48 - Definitions The Prebisch-Singer Hypothesis, The Lewis Model, Industrialisation.html

items.answer: As world incomes rise, countries who depend on exporting primary products will see a deterioration in their terms of trade.

As countries industrialise, manufacturing firms will make more profit and increase investment. This will increase their demand for labour which will increase wages and attract even more workers into the manufacturing sector.
Where the main industry in a country shifts from agriculture to manufacturing.

definitions.title: World Bank, IMF and NGOs
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/49 - World Bank, IMF and NGOs.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/49 - World Bank, IMF and NGOs.html

definitions.id: 50 - Definitions 1 World Bank, IMF and NGOs
definitions.title: Definitions 1: World Bank, IMF and NGOs
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/50 - Definitions 1 World Bank, IMF and NGOs.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/50 - Definitions 1 World Bank, IMF and NGOs.html

items.answer: A bank for countries which promotes development by offering development loans and debt relief.
An organisation which monitors the global economy to ensure that it is healthy and stable. They occasionally offer bailouts to countries in need.
Help to promote development by focusing on smaller-scale, specific issues.

definitions.id: 51 - Definitions Role of Financial Markets, Equity, Forward Contracts
definitions.title: Definitions: Role of Financial Markets, Equity, Forward Contracts
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/51 - Definitions Role of Financial Markets, Equity, Forward Contracts.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/51 - Definitions Role of Financial Markets, Equity, Forward Contracts.html

items.answer: To facilitate saving, to facilitate lending, to facilitate exchange, to provide a market for equities and to provide forward markets.
When firms sell a percentage of their company to investors in the form of shares. The investors can then receive a percentage of the profits.
Fix the price and date of a future transaction now so that you know exactly how much you will pay.

definitions.title: Definitions: Subprime Mortgages, Housing Bubble
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/52 - Definitions Subprime Mortgages, Housing Bubble.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/52 - Definitions Subprime Mortgages, Housing Bubble.html

items.answer: Mortgages given to people who couldn’t afford to pay them back.
As house prices increase, demand for houses increases. This increases house prices further and the cycle continues until houses become hugely overvalued.

definitions.id: 53 - Definitions Financial Market Failure, Market Bubble, Moral Hazard
definitions.title: Definitions: Financial Market Failure, Market Bubble, Moral Hazard
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/53 - Definitions Financial Market Failure, Market Bubble, Moral Hazard.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/53 - Definitions Financial Market Failure, Market Bubble, Moral Hazard.html

items.answer: Five types of financial market failure are; asymmetric information, speculation and market bubbles, negative externalities, moral hazard and market rigging.
As the price of an asset increases, demand for that asset increases. This increases the price of the asset further and the cycle continues until the asset become hugely overvalued.
When someone takes more risks because somebody else is bearing the cost of that risk.
Market rigging occurs when firms unfairly try to control prices, distorting the price mechanism.

definitions.id: 54 - Definitions Progressive Tax, Regressive Tax, Proportional Tax
definitions.title: Definitions: Progressive Tax, Regressive Tax, Proportional Tax
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/54 - Definitions Progressive Tax, Regressive Tax, Proportional Tax.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/54 - Definitions Progressive Tax, Regressive Tax, Proportional Tax.html

items.answer: With a progressive tax, the proportion of income, profit or price you pay in tax will increase as your income, profit or price increases.
With a regressive tax, the proportion of income, profit or price you pay in tax will decrease as your income, profit or price increases.
With a proportional tax, the proportion of income, profit or price you pay in tax will stay the same as your income, profit or price increases.

definitions.id: 55 - Definitions Capital Expenditure, Current Expenditure, Transfer Payments
definitions.title: Definitions: Capital Expenditure, Current Expenditure, Transfer Payments
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/55 - Definitions Capital Expenditure, Current Expenditure, Transfer Payments.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/55 - Definitions Capital Expenditure, Current Expenditure, Transfer Payments.html

items.answer: Government spending on assets with a long-term return e.g. hospitals
Government spending on recurring costs e.g. wages
Government spending for which nothing is received in return e.g. benefits

definitions.id: 56 - Definitions Resource Crowding Out, Financial Crowding Out
definitions.title: Definitions: Resource Crowding Out, Financial Crowding Out
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/56 - Definitions Resource Crowding Out, Financial Crowding Out.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/56 - Definitions Resource Crowding Out, Financial Crowding Out.html

items.answer: Resource crowding out is when public expenditure reduces resources available for the private sector.
Financial crowding out is when government borrowing pushes up the interest rate which decreases private sector investment.

definitions.id: 57 - Definitions Budget Deficit, Cyclical Deficit and Structural Deficit
definitions.title: Definitions: Budget Deficit, Cyclical Deficit and Structural Deficit
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/57 - Definitions Budget Deficit, Cyclical Deficit and Structural Deficit.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/57 - Definitions Budget Deficit, Cyclical Deficit and Structural Deficit.html

items.answer: When public expenditure is greater than tax revenue.
A cyclical deficit occurs during a recession when public expenditure is high and tax revenues are low.
A structural deficit is the budget deficit that remains at any point of the business cycle.

definitions.title: Definitions: National Debt
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/58 - Definitions National Debt.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/58 - Definitions National Debt.html

items.answer: National debt is the total debt built up by government borrowing over time.

definitions.id: 59 - Definitions Discretionary Fiscal Policy, Automatic Stabilisers
definitions.title: Definitions: Discretionary Fiscal Policy, Automatic Stabilisers
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/59 - Definitions Discretionary Fiscal Policy, Automatic Stabilisers.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/59 - Definitions Discretionary Fiscal Policy, Automatic Stabilisers.html

items.answer: When a government makes decisions to actively change tax and public expenditure.
When tax and public expenditure changes happen without government intervention and help to keep the economy stable.

definitions.id: 60 - Definitions Expansionary Monetary Policy, Contractionary Monetary Policy, Interventionist Supply-Side Policy, Market-Based Supply-Side Polic
definitions.title: Definitions: Expansionary Monetary Policy, Contractionary Monetary Policy, Interventionist Supply-Side Policy, Market-Based Supply-Side Policy
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/60 - Definitions Expansionary Monetary Policy, Contractionary Monetary Policy, Interventionist Supply-Side Policy, Market-Based Supply-Side Polic.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/60 - Definitions Expansionary Monetary Policy, Contractionary Monetary Policy, Interventionist Supply-Side Policy, Market-Based Supply-Side Polic.html

items.answer: Expansionary (or loose) monetary policy is when the base interest rate decreases or when the central bank uses quantitative easing in order to expand the economy.
Contractionary (or tight) monetary policy is when the base interest rate increases in order to contract the economy!
Interventionist supply-side policy is when the government intervenes, when it is actively involved in increasing aggregate supply.
Market-based supply-side policy is when the government decides to reduce intervention and let the market do its thing.

definitions.id: 61 - Definitions Exchange Rate Policy and Direct Controls
definitions.title: Definitions: Exchange Rate Policy and Direct Controls
definitions.jsonPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/61 - Definitions Exchange Rate Policy and Direct Controls.json
definitions.htmlPath: archive/UpLearn Economics/Year 13/17 - Macroeconomics Theme 4/Definitions/61 - Definitions Exchange Rate Policy and Direct Controls.html

items.answer: Exchange rate policy manages exchange rates by changing either base interest rates, or through foreign currency transactions.
Direct controls are any policies which work outside of market forces, which directly intervene to tightly control parts of an economy. One common example is the minimum wage.
